meta.name: Event Loop with epoll
meta.description: Single-threaded server handling 10K+ connections using epoll and the reactor pattern
meta.essence: Scalable I/O multiplexing using the reactor pattern where a single-threaded event loop uses epoll to monitor thousands of file descriptors, dispatches events to registered handlers, and manages connection state machines with non-blocking I/O, write buffering, and timer-based idle timeouts.
meta.why_important: Building this teaches the foundational architecture behind NGINX, Redis, and Node.js, demonstrating how systems achieve massive concurrency without threads through non-blocking I/O and event-driven programming.
learning_outcomes: Implement epoll-based I/O multiplexing understanding edge-triggered vs level-triggered semantics
Design a reactor pattern event loop with per-fd state management and callback dispatch
Handle non-blocking socket operations with EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK, write buffering, and EPOLLOUT management
Build timer management for connection idle timeouts
Implement an incremental HTTP parser for streaming request data

milestones.name: epoll Basics: Level-Triggered and Edge-Triggered
milestones.description: Set up epoll-based event loop with non-blocking sockets. Implement both level-triggered (LT) and edge-triggered (ET) modes, understanding the critical difference in read/write handling.
acceptance_criteria: Create epoll instance with epoll_create1(EPOLL_CLOEXEC) and register the listening socket for EPOLLIN events
Set ALL sockets (listening and client) to non-blocking mode using fcntl(O_NONBLOCK)
Accept new connections in a loop (accept until EAGAIN) and register each with epoll
Implement level-triggered (LT) mode first: epoll re-notifies if data remains unread; read can process one chunk per event
Then implement edge-triggered (ET) mode (EPOLLET flag): read in a loop until EAGAIN to drain the socket completely, because epoll will NOT re-notify for already-available data
Demonstrate the difference: in ET mode, reading only once per event loses data; in LT mode, it works but may cause redundant wakeups
Handle EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK on all non-blocking read() and write() calls without treating them as errors
Build a basic echo server as the test application: read data from client, echo it back
Use an array or hash map indexed by file descriptor to store per-connection state
pitfalls: Edge-triggered mode REQUIRES reading until EAGAIN; a single read per event will lose data if more than one read-worth of data arrives between epoll_wait calls
Level-triggered mode can cause a thundering-herd-like issue where the same fd is reported as ready on every epoll_wait call if data isn't fully consumed
The listening socket must also be non-blocking; otherwise accept() blocks when a connection is initiated and then reset before accept runs (the 'accept race')
epoll_wait maxevents parameter should be sized reasonably (e.g., 1024); too small causes extra epoll_wait calls, too large wastes stack
Not setting EPOLL_CLOEXEC on the epoll fd leaks it to child processes
deliverables: epoll instance creation and listening socket registration
Non-blocking socket configuration for all connections
Level-triggered event loop with single-read-per-event
Edge-triggered event loop with drain-until-EAGAIN loop
Echo server demonstrating both modes
Per-fd state map for connection tracking

milestones.name: Write Buffering and Timer Management
milestones.description: Implement write buffer management with EPOLLOUT backpressure handling and timer-based connection idle timeouts.
acceptance_criteria: When write() returns EAGAIN (socket send buffer full), buffer the remaining data in a per-connection write queue
Register interest in EPOLLOUT for that fd; when EPOLLOUT fires, attempt to flush the write buffer
When the write buffer is fully flushed, DEREGISTER EPOLLOUT interest to avoid busy-spinning on write-ready events
Handle partial writes: write() may send fewer bytes than requested; advance the buffer offset and retry on next EPOLLOUT
Implement timer management using a min-heap (priority queue) or single-level timer wheel with configurable resolution
Support connection idle timeouts: if no data is received for N seconds, close the connection and free its state
Use epoll_wait timeout parameter set to the time until the next timer expiration; when no timers are pending, use -1 (infinite wait)
Timer cancellation: when a connection receives data, reset its idle timer; when a connection closes, cancel its timer
pitfalls: Leaving EPOLLOUT registered when the write buffer is empty causes epoll_wait to return immediately every iteration (busy loop), consuming 100% CPU
Timer wheel slot count should be a power of 2 for efficient modulo (bitwise AND); non-power-of-2 requires expensive division
epoll_wait timeout is in milliseconds; timer resolution finer than 1ms is not achievable with epoll alone
Closing a connection must cancel its timer AND deregister from epoll AND free per-connection buffers; missing any step leaks resources
Multiple timer expirations may occur in a single tick; must process all expired timers, not just one
concepts: Min-heap or timer wheel for timeout management
skills: Write buffer management with partial write handling
deliverables: Per-connection write buffer with partial write tracking
EPOLLOUT registration on EAGAIN, deregistration on buffer flush
Timer data structure (min-heap or timer wheel) with insert, cancel, and expire-all operations
Idle timeout enforcement closing inactive connections
epoll_wait timeout derived from next timer expiration

milestones.name: Reactor API and Callback Dispatch
milestones.description: Abstract the raw epoll calls into a clean reactor-pattern API with event registration, callback dispatch, and deferred task scheduling.
acceptance_criteria: Clean API for registering interest in fd events: reactor.register(fd, READABLE | WRITABLE, callback); reactor.deregister(fd)
Callback functions receive the fd and event type (readable, writable, error/hangup) as arguments
Support one-shot and repeating timer callbacks: reactor.set_timeout(ms, callback) and reactor.set_interval(ms, callback)
Deferred task queue: reactor.defer(callback) schedules a function to run after all I/O events in the current tick are processed
Handle callback re-entrancy: a callback that calls reactor.deregister(fd) or reactor.register(fd) must not corrupt the current iteration's event list; defer modifications to the next epoll_wait call
EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR events are dispatched to the registered callback with appropriate event type flags
API hides epoll internals: user code never calls epoll_ctl or epoll_wait directly
pitfalls: Modifying the epoll interest set during event dispatch (inside a callback) can invalidate the current events array; must defer epoll_ctl calls or copy the events array
Deferred tasks should run after ALL I/O events in the current tick, not interleaved with them
A callback that closes an fd must not cause a use-after-free if that fd has a pending event in the same epoll_wait batch; must mark closed fds and skip their events
Re-entrancy: a timer callback that sets another timer must not corrupt the timer data structure during iteration
concepts: Safe event set modification during dispatch
deliverables: Event dispatch loop calling registered callbacks
Deferred task queue running after I/O dispatch
Safe fd closure during event dispatch (deferred deregistration)
EPOLLHUP/EPOLLERR event handling

milestones.name: HTTP Server on Event Loop
milestones.description: Build a complete HTTP/1.1 server on top of the reactor API, demonstrating incremental parsing, per-connection state machines, keep-alive, and C10K capability.
acceptance_criteria: Parse HTTP requests incrementally as data arrives: accumulate bytes in a per-connection read buffer; attempt to parse after each read; handle requests that span multiple read events
Per-connection state machine with states: READING_HEADERS, READING_BODY, PROCESSING, WRITING_RESPONSE, CLOSING
Handle partial writes using the write buffer from M2: if the response can't be sent in one write, buffer and register EPOLLOUT
Support HTTP/1.1 keep-alive: after sending a response, reset the connection state to READING_HEADERS for the next request (unless Connection: close)
Idle timeout (from M2): connections with no activity for 30 seconds are closed
Serve static files from a configured directory with correct Content-Type and Content-Length
Benchmark with a load testing tool (e.g., wrk, ab): demonstrate 10K+ concurrent connections with p99 latency under 100ms for static file responses
Connection cleanup on error or timeout: free read buffer, write buffer, cancel timer, deregister from epoll, close fd
pitfalls: HTTP request headers may arrive split across multiple read() calls; the parser must handle partial headers gracefully (not assume the full request is in one read)
The write buffer can grow unboundedly if the client reads slowly (slow loris); must set a maximum write buffer size and close connections that exceed it
Keep-alive connections accumulate over time; without idle timeouts the server runs out of file descriptors
Pipelining: HTTP/1.1 allows clients to send multiple requests without waiting for responses; the server must process them in order
Closing a connection from a timer callback while an EPOLLIN event is pending for the same fd causes use-after-free; must handle via deferred close (M3)
skills: Resource cleanup under all exit paths
deliverables: Incremental HTTP request parser accumulating across reads
Per-connection state machine (read headers → read body → process → write → keep-alive/close)
Write buffer integration with EPOLLOUT backpressure from M2
HTTP keep-alive with Connection header support
Static file serving with MIME types
Resource cleanup on close/error/timeout

blueprint.title: Event Loop with epoll
blueprint.overview:
This project builds a production-grade, single-threaded server capable of handling 10,000+ simultaneous connections — the legendary C10K target — using Linux's epoll interface and the Reactor pattern. You will construct the exact architectural foundation that powers NGINX, Redis, Node.js, and virtually every high-performance network server in existence. The journey starts at raw file descriptors and syscalls, moves through write buffering and timer management, abstracts everything into a clean Reactor API, and culminates in a benchmarked HTTP/1.1 server.

The central insight this project drives home is deceptively simple but profound: threads are not the right primitive for I/O concurrency. A single thread, armed with epoll and non-blocking sockets, can monitor tens of thousands of file descriptors simultaneously, waking only when work actually exists. This is not a trick — it is a fundamentally different concurrency model where the OS kernel becomes your scheduler for I/O readiness, and your event loop becomes the coordinator of all asynchronous state machines.

You will implement the complete stack from scratch: epoll creation, edge-triggered vs. level-triggered semantics, per-connection state machines, write buffering with EPOLLOUT backpressure, a timer heap for idle timeouts, a clean Reactor abstraction with deferred task scheduling, and finally an incremental HTTP/1.1 parser with keep-alive support. Every layer builds on the one before it, and by the end you will be able to read NGINX's source code and understand every design decision it makes.
blueprint.design_philosophy: This project teaches by forcing you to negotiate with the kernel's I/O readiness model at every step. The hardware reality — that sockets are full-duplex byte streams backed by kernel ring buffers of fixed size — means that every read and write is potentially partial, potentially blocking, and potentially lost if you misread the notification semantics. There is no framework hiding this from you. You see the raw EAGAIN, you manage the write queue, you cancel the timer on close. This friction is the lesson. Once you understand why EPOLLOUT must be deregistered when the write buffer empties (to prevent a 100% CPU busy loop), you will never again wonder why Node.js's write() returns a boolean for backpressure, or why Redis buffers commands before flushing. The Reactor pattern is the clean abstraction over this chaos, and building it yourself means you own the mental model permanently.

implementation.rationale: The core challenge is direct negotiation with Linux kernel interfaces: epoll_create1, epoll_ctl, epoll_wait, fcntl, accept4, non-blocking read/write, and file descriptor lifecycle. C provides zero-overhead access to these syscalls with explicit control over memory layout (per-fd state structs, write buffer ring buffers, min-heap nodes). Rust is viable but adds lifetime complexity that distracts from the epoll learning objectives at intermediate level. Go hides the very thing being learned — its runtime wraps epoll internally.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for all identifiers. Structs named as noun phrases (conn_state_t, reactor_t, timer_heap_t). Functions prefixed by module (reactor_register, conn_close, timer_insert). Error handling via negative return values (errno-style). All public APIs documented with parameter semantics in header files. No global mutable state except the single reactor instance. Memory ownership explicitly documented in comments (caller-allocates, callee-frees pattern). Compile with -Wall -Wextra -Werror -O2 -g.
implementation.build_system: Makefile with targets: all, clean, test, bench. Separate object files per translation unit. CFLAGS includes -D_GNU_SOURCE for epoll extensions.
alternatives: Rust — excellent choice for production due to ownership model preventing use-after-free on fd close; mio crate provides epoll wrapper; adds complexity for intermediate learners
Go — possible but counterproductive since Go's runtime wraps epoll, hiding the exact mechanisms being taught

assumed_known: POSIX socket API: socket(), bind(), listen(), accept(), connect()
Basic TCP/IP concepts: three-way handshake, half-open connections
File descriptors and the Unix everything-is-a-file model
Completed a basic blocking HTTP server (prerequisite project http-server-basic)

must_teach_first.concept: Kernel socket buffer model: send buffer + receive buffer, how they fill and drain, what EAGAIN means physically
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 1 — before explaining ET vs LT

must_teach_first.concept: The difference between I/O readiness notification and I/O completion notification (readiness = epoll/select; completion = io_uring/IOCP)
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 1 — frames why epoll works the way it does

must_teach_first.concept: File descriptor table internals: FD is an index into per-process table pointing to kernel file description (shared on fork/dup); epoll watches kernel file descriptions not FD numbers
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 1 — explains EPOLL_CLOEXEC and fd duplication pitfalls

must_teach_first.concept: The Reactor vs Proactor pattern distinction — Reactor dispatches on readiness, Proactor dispatches on completion
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 3 — before abstracting the Reactor API

must_teach_first.concept: Min-heap data structure: insert O(log n), extract-min O(log n), decrease-key O(log n) — needed for timer management
HTTP/1.1 keep-alive, pipelining, Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding: chunked, and the Connection header
must_teach_first.when: Milestone 4 — before building the HTTP state machine

milestones.title: epoll Basics: Level-Triggered and Edge-Triggered
milestones.summary: Stand up an epoll-based event loop that accepts thousands of non-blocking connections. Implement and compare level-triggered (LT) and edge-triggered (ET) notification semantics. Build an echo server that demonstrates both modes and correctly handles EAGAIN on all non-blocking I/O calls. Establish per-fd state management via an array or hash map indexed by file descriptor number.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume epoll is just a faster select() — a single syscall that returns 'which fds are ready', and that you just read once per notification. Many also assume edge-triggered is simply 'better' than level-triggered and should always be used.
milestones.reveal: epoll is a kernel-managed interest set with a separate readiness queue. The distinction between LT and ET is about WHEN the kernel places an fd back into the readiness queue: LT re-notifies on every epoll_wait if data remains; ET notifies exactly once per state transition. This means ET mode is not 'faster' — it is a completely different contract that REQUIRES draining the socket to EAGAIN, or you silently lose events. Choosing ET incorrectly causes a class of bugs — data sitting unread in the kernel buffer while your server waits for events that will never come — that is nearly impossible to detect under low load.
cascade: Non-blocking I/O universally — EAGAIN is not an error; it is the kernel's way of saying 'come back later'. This same semantic appears in pipes, timerfd, eventfd, signalfd, and io_uring's non-blocking path.
Node.js libuv uses ET mode epoll internally — now you understand why Node.js events are 'fire and forget' and why a CPU-heavy callback can starve I/O even in an 'async' system.

The thundering herd problem (cross-domain: distributed systems) — LT mode on a shared accept socket causes all worker processes to wake when one connection arrives; Linux's EPOLLEXCLUSIVE flag and SO_REUSEPORT both address this, for the same reason ET mode exists.

Database B-tree page reads — the same 'drain until empty' loop pattern appears in buffered I/O: a database reading a page from disk in a loop until a full page is assembled, handling partial reads from the OS.

The accept() race condition — a connection that arrives and resets before accept() runs leaves a phantom connection in the kernel backlog; non-blocking listen sockets handle this gracefully by returning ECONNABORTED.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Create epoll instance with epoll_create1(EPOLL_CLOEXEC) and register the listening socket for EPOLLIN events
Set ALL sockets (listening and client) to non-blocking mode using fcntl(O_NONBLOCK)
Accept new connections in a loop (accept until EAGAIN) and register each with epoll
Implement level-triggered (LT) mode first: epoll re-notifies if data remains unread; read can process one chunk per event
Then implement edge-triggered (ET) mode (EPOLLET flag): read in a loop until EAGAIN to drain the socket completely, because epoll will NOT re-notify for already-available data
Demonstrate the difference: in ET mode, reading only once per event loses data; in LT mode, it works but may cause redundant wakeups
Handle EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK on all non-blocking read() and write() calls without treating them as errors
Build a basic echo server as the test application: read data from client, echo it back
Use an array or hash map indexed by file descriptor to store per-connection state

milestones.title: Write Buffering and Timer Management
milestones.summary: Implement the two critical housekeeping systems that every production event loop requires. First: write buffering with EPOLLOUT backpressure — when a write returns EAGAIN because the kernel send buffer is full, buffer the remaining bytes locally and register EPOLLOUT interest, then drain and deregister when the buffer empties. Second: a timer subsystem using a min-heap (priority queue) that integrates with epoll_wait's timeout parameter to enforce connection idle timeouts without a separate thread.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume write() either succeeds fully or fails with an error. Many also assume timers need a background thread or signal (SIGALRM). A common pattern is to leave EPOLLOUT always registered 'just in case' a write needs it.
milestones.reveal: write() on a non-blocking socket can return anywhere from 0 to len bytes without error — a partial write is a success. The remaining bytes must be queued or they are silently dropped. Leaving EPOLLOUT permanently registered is catastrophic: when the send buffer has space (which is almost always), epoll_wait returns immediately on every call, burning 100% CPU in a tight loop with no work to do. The EPOLLOUT lifecycle — register on EAGAIN, deregister on buffer drain — is the critical invariant. For timers, epoll_wait's timeout parameter IS the timer mechanism: computing 'milliseconds until next expiry' and passing it as the timeout gives you timer callbacks without any threads or signals.
cascade: TCP flow control and backpressure (cross-domain: distributed systems) — EAGAIN on write IS TCP backpressure propagating up from the receiver's advertised window through the kernel send buffer to your application. Redis's client output buffer limits and Node.js's writable.write() returning false are both this same mechanism.
Slow loris attack — a client that reads slowly causes the server's write buffer to grow without bound; the maximum write buffer size limit you'll add in M4 is the defense.
timerfd_create — an alternative to epoll_wait timeout integration that makes timers first-class epoll events; used by systemd and some game servers for higher-precision timers.

min-heap universally — the same data structure powers: OS process scheduler (CFS uses a red-black tree, a more complex cousin), Go's runtime timer heap, Java's PriorityQueue, Dijkstra's algorithm. Understanding it once gives you insight everywhere.

Timer wheel (cross-domain: networking) — Linux's kernel timer implementation uses a hierarchical timer wheel instead of a min-heap for O(1) insert/delete at the cost of fixed resolution. Understanding why epoll_wait timeout only gives millisecond resolution explains why high-frequency trading systems use HPET or TSC-based timers.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: When write() returns EAGAIN (socket send buffer full), buffer the remaining data in a per-connection write queue
Register interest in EPOLLOUT for that fd; when EPOLLOUT fires, attempt to flush the write buffer
When the write buffer is fully flushed, DEREGISTER EPOLLOUT interest to avoid busy-spinning on write-ready events
Handle partial writes: write() may send fewer bytes than requested; advance the buffer offset and retry on next EPOLLOUT
Implement timer management using a min-heap (priority queue) or single-level timer wheel with configurable resolution
Support connection idle timeouts: if no data is received for N seconds, close the connection and free its state
Use epoll_wait timeout parameter set to the time until the next timer expiration; when no timers are pending, use -1 (infinite wait)
Timer cancellation: when a connection receives data, reset its idle timer; when a connection closes, cancel its timer

milestones.title: Reactor API and Callback Dispatch
milestones.summary: Abstract all raw epoll machinery behind a clean Reactor pattern API: reactor_register(), reactor_deregister(), reactor_set_timeout(), reactor_set_interval(), and reactor_defer(). The Reactor becomes the sole caller of epoll_ctl and epoll_wait; user code only provides callbacks. Solve the critical re-entrancy problem: callbacks that modify registration state (deregister, close) during the dispatch loop must not corrupt the current event iteration — solved via a deferred modification queue that executes after all events in the current tick are dispatched.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume that once you have a working epoll loop, wrapping it in an API is cosmetic. They also assume that a callback can safely call reactor_deregister() and that the reactor will 'just handle it' — not realizing they are modifying the data structure being iterated.
milestones.reveal: The Reactor API design is where the real intellectual work lives. The deferred task queue and the 'mark-and-skip' pattern for closed fds during dispatch are non-trivial safety requirements. Without them, a callback that closes a connection causes the dispatch loop to process a subsequent event on that same fd — reading from freed memory or a recycled file descriptor. This is the event loop equivalent of iterator invalidation. The deferred queue also enables microtask-like semantics: work that must run after all I/O but before the next epoll_wait, which is exactly how Node.js's process.nextTick() works.
cascade: Node.js event loop phases (cross-domain: JavaScript runtime) — now you understand precisely why Node.js has separate phases (timers, I/O callbacks, setImmediate, nextTick) and why process.nextTick() runs before setImmediate(). Each phase maps to a section of the Reactor loop you just built.
libuv's uv_run() — the function you are building. libuv's source code will now be directly readable; you can trace every handle type to an epoll registration.

NGINX worker event loop — NGINX's ngx_process_events_and_timers() is structurally identical to the loop you are building: epoll_wait, dispatch read/write callbacks, process timer heap, run deferred post-handlers.

Safe iterator invalidation (cross-domain: data structures) — the 'copy events array, then process' pattern you use here is the same reason Java's CopyOnWriteArrayList exists and why Rust's borrow checker prevents iterator invalidation at compile time.

Proactor pattern comparison — once you own the Reactor, you can understand why Windows IOCP (Proactor) dispatches on completion rather than readiness, and why io_uring is a Linux attempt to bring Proactor semantics to Linux.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Clean API for registering interest in fd events: reactor.register(fd, READABLE | WRITABLE, callback); reactor.deregister(fd)
Callback functions receive the fd and event type (readable, writable, error/hangup) as arguments
Support one-shot and repeating timer callbacks: reactor.set_timeout(ms, callback) and reactor.set_interval(ms, callback)
Deferred task queue: reactor.defer(callback) schedules a function to run after all I/O events in the current tick are processed
Handle callback re-entrancy: a callback that calls reactor.deregister(fd) or reactor.register(fd) must not corrupt the current iteration's event list; defer modifications to the next epoll_wait call
EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR events are dispatched to the registered callback with appropriate event type flags
API hides epoll internals: user code never calls epoll_ctl or epoll_wait directly

milestones.title: HTTP Server on Event Loop
milestones.summary: Build a complete, benchmarked HTTP/1.1 server on top of the Reactor API from M3. The HTTP layer implements an incremental request parser that assembles headers across multiple EPOLLIN events, a per-connection state machine (READING_HEADERS → READING_BODY → PROCESSING → WRITING_RESPONSE → KEEP_ALIVE/CLOSING), HTTP keep-alive with Connection header support, static file serving with MIME types, and full resource cleanup on all exit paths. Benchmark with wrk or ab to demonstrate 10K+ concurrent connections with p99 latency under 100ms.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume HTTP is a simple request-response protocol and that parsing a request means reading it all at once with fgets() or recv(). Many also assume that 10K connections requires multi-threading or at least a thread pool.
milestones.reveal: HTTP headers in the real world routinely arrive split across multiple TCP segments — a 2KB header block may arrive as three separate read() calls returning 800, 900, and 300 bytes. The parser must be a true state machine that can be suspended and resumed between reads, accumulating bytes until a complete logical unit (the blank line ending headers, or Content-Length bytes of body) is assembled. The 10K connection benchmark with a single thread works because 99.9% of those connections are idle at any given microsecond — you are not computing for 10K connections simultaneously, you are managing state for 10K connections and computing for whichever handful have events right now.
cascade: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 frame parsing (cross-domain: protocol design) — the incremental parser you build for HTTP/1.1 header parsing is the same architectural pattern used for HTTP/2 frame parsing, QUIC packet parsing, and TLS record layer parsing. Every streaming protocol needs a resumable parser.
Redis protocol (RESP) — Redis's inline command and bulk string parser is a simpler version of exactly what you built. Its source code (networking.c) will now be directly readable.

Slow loris defense — your maximum write buffer limit directly defends against slow loris and similar resource exhaustion attacks. This is why NGINX has client_body_timeout and client_header_timeout configurations.

The C10K problem historically — in 1999, 10K connections was considered impossible on commodity hardware. Understanding that it required rethinking from threads to events explains the entire decade of server architecture evolution (Apache prefork → NGINX events → Node.js).

sendfile() syscall (cross-domain: OS zero-copy) — static file serving with read()+write() copies data kernel→userspace→kernel. sendfile() eliminates the round trip. This is how NGINX achieves zero-copy file serving and why it is dramatically faster than Apache for static files — a direct extension of what you built here.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Parse HTTP requests incrementally as data arrives: accumulate bytes in a per-connection read buffer; attempt to parse after each read; handle requests that span multiple read events
Per-connection state machine with states: READING_HEADERS, READING_BODY, PROCESSING, WRITING_RESPONSE, CLOSING
Handle partial writes using the write buffer from M2: if the response can't be sent in one write, buffer and register EPOLLOUT
Support HTTP/1.1 keep-alive: after sending a response, reset the connection state to READING_HEADERS for the next request (unless Connection: close)
Idle timeout (from M2): connections with no activity for 30 seconds are closed
Serve static files from a configured directory with correct Content-Type and Content-Length
Benchmark with a load testing tool (e.g., wrk, ab): demonstrate 10K+ concurrent connections with p99 latency under 100ms for static file responses
Connection cleanup on error or timeout: free read buffer, write buffer, cancel timer, deregister from epoll, close fd

diagrams.title: L0 Satellite: Project-Wide System Map
diagrams.description: Full project overview showing all four milestones as interconnected layers: M1 (epoll core) feeds into M2 (write buffer + timers), both feed into M3 (Reactor API abstraction), which M4 (HTTP server) sits on top of. Shows the hardware-to-application stack: NIC → kernel socket buffers → epoll interest set → readiness queue → event loop → connection state machines → HTTP responses. Arrows show data flow and control flow separately. Every milestone anchor ID appears as a node.

diagrams.title: Kernel File Descriptor and Socket Buffer Model
diagrams.description: Three-level view: (L1) user code calls read(fd, buf, n) → (L2) kernel looks up fd in process FD table → kernel file description → socket struct → receive ring buffer → copies bytes to user buffer → (L3) NIC DMA'd packet data into socket ring buffer via NAPI interrupt. Shows the physical path data travels from wire to application buffer. Explains WHY non-blocking sockets return EAGAIN: the receive buffer is empty, there are no bytes to copy.

diagrams.title: epoll Interest Set vs. Readiness Queue (Kernel Data Structures)
diagrams.description: Shows the two kernel data structures epoll maintains: (1) the interest set — a red-black tree of (fd, events) pairs registered via epoll_ctl ADD/MOD/DEL; (2) the readiness list — a linked list of fds that have pending events, drained by epoll_wait. Arrows show: data arrives on fd → kernel checks interest set → if fd is in interest set and event matches → adds to readiness list → epoll_wait returns it. Shows why epoll scales: interest set lookup is O(log n) via the red-black tree, but epoll_wait only returns actually-ready fds.

diagrams.title: Level-Triggered vs. Edge-Triggered: Kernel Notification State Machine
diagrams.description: Side-by-side state diagrams for LT and ET modes. LT: fd with data → added to readiness list → epoll_wait returns it → if data still remains after handler returns → fd stays on readiness list → returned again next epoll_wait. ET: fd transitions from no-data to has-data → added to readiness list exactly once → epoll_wait returns it → fd removed from readiness list regardless of remaining data → NOT re-added until next state transition (new data arrives). Shows the 'data stranded in buffer' failure mode when ET handler reads only once.

diagrams.title: Edge-Triggered Drain Loop: Correct vs. Incorrect Control Flow
diagrams.description: Before/after code flow diagram. WRONG path: EPOLLIN event → read(fd, buf, 4096) once → return. Shows kernel buffer with 8192 bytes remaining, next epoll_wait returns nothing, connection stalls. CORRECT path: EPOLLIN event → while(true) { n = read(fd, buf, 4096); if n == -1 and errno == EAGAIN → break; if n == 0 → close connection; process bytes } → kernel buffer fully drained → epoll will notify on next new data arrival. Annotates each branch with the syscall return value and errno.

diagrams.title: Accept Loop: Draining New Connections Under Load
diagrams.description: Shows the listening socket EPOLLIN event handling. Without accept loop: one connection accepted per event → at 10K connections/sec, epoll_wait is called 10K times/sec with 1 accept each → overhead. With accept loop: while((fd = accept4(...)) != -1) { register with epoll } → drains all pending connections in one epoll_wait iteration. Shows the EAGAIN termination condition. Illustrates the accept race: connection arrives → TCP RST before accept → accept4 returns ECONNABORTED → non-blocking mode handles gracefully.

diagrams.title: Per-FD State Array Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Structure layout diagram showing conn_state_t struct with byte offsets: [0] int fd (4B), [4] int state (4B), [8] uint8_t* read_buf (8B), [16] size_t read_buf_len (8B), [24] uint8_t* write_buf (8B), [32] size_t write_buf_len (8B), [40] size_t write_buf_offset (8B), [48] timer_id_t timer_id (8B). Total: 56 bytes per connection. Array indexed by fd number. Shows cache line analysis: first 64 bytes (one cache line) covers all hot fields accessed on every event. Contrast with hash map: pointer chasing = cache miss per lookup.

diagrams.title: Write Buffer Lifecycle: EAGAIN → Buffer → EPOLLOUT → Drain
diagrams.description: State transition diagram for per-connection write path. States: (1) WRITE_DIRECT: write() called, bytes sent, write_buf empty, EPOLLOUT not registered. (2) WRITE_BUFFERED: write() returned EAGAIN or partial — remaining bytes copied to write_buf, EPOLLOUT registered via epoll_ctl MOD. (3) FLUSHING: EPOLLOUT event fires, write() called on write_buf, may be partial again. (4) FLUSH_COMPLETE: write_buf empty, EPOLLOUT deregistered via epoll_ctl MOD back to EPOLLIN only. Annotates every epoll_ctl call with its events mask.

diagrams.title: EPOLLOUT Always-Registered: The Busy Loop Anti-Pattern
diagrams.description: Before/after CPU utilization diagram. BEFORE (EPOLLOUT always registered): epoll_wait returns immediately (send buffer has space) → no data to write → loop again → epoll_wait returns immediately → 100% CPU, 0% useful work. Timeline shows epoll_wait returning ~1M times/sec with EPOLLOUT, burning a core. AFTER (EPOLLOUT registered only when write_buf non-empty): epoll_wait blocks until data arrives → process data → return to wait. Timeline shows epoll_wait waking only on actual events. Annotates with actual CPU% numbers from perf stat output.

diagrams.title: Partial Write Buffer Offset Tracking
diagrams.description: Memory layout diagram showing write_buf as a flat byte array with write_buf_len (total bytes queued) and write_buf_offset (bytes already sent). Shows write() call returning n < remaining → offset advances by n → next EPOLLOUT → write(&write_buf[offset], len - offset) → continues until offset == len → buffer cleared, EPOLLOUT deregistered. Shows the memmove() optimization (shift bytes to front) vs the offset pointer approach (no copy, preferred). Annotates with: if offset > len/2, memmove to reclaim space.

diagrams.title: Min-Heap Timer Data Structure: Memory Layout and Operations
diagrams.description: Array-backed binary min-heap diagram. Shows heap array with timer_node_t elements: {expiry_ms (uint64_t), conn_id (int), cancelled (bool)}. Heap property: heap[i].expiry <= heap[2i+1].expiry and heap[2i+2].expiry. Shows parent/child index arithmetic (parent = (i-1)/2, children = 2i+1, 2i+2). Diagrams INSERT operation: append + sift-up. Diagrams EXTRACT-MIN: swap root with last element + sift-down. Shows CANCEL operation via lazy deletion (cancelled=true, skip on extract). Annotates each operation with O(log n) complexity for n=10K timers.

diagrams.title: epoll_wait Timeout as Timer Mechanism
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing three scenarios: (1) No timers: epoll_wait(-1) → blocks indefinitely until I/O event. (2) Timer in 5000ms, no I/O: epoll_wait(5000) → kernel wakes at exactly expiry → returns 0 events → process expired timers. (3) Timer in 5000ms, I/O event at 2000ms: epoll_wait(5000) → wakes at 2000ms with events → process I/O → recompute timeout = 3000ms → epoll_wait(3000). Shows timeout computation: timeout_ms = max(0, heap_min_expiry - now_ms). Annotates clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) for monotonic time.

diagrams.title: Full Connection Lifecycle with Timers
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram: accept4() → create conn_state_t → epoll_ctl ADD EPOLLIN → timer_insert(now+30s, conn_id) → [data arrives] → read() → timer_cancel(conn_id) → timer_insert(now+30s, conn_id) → [30s no data] → timer fires → close_conn(conn_id) → epoll_ctl DEL → free read_buf → free write_buf → close(fd). Shows all four cleanup steps that MUST happen on close and the order they must occur in (epoll DEL before close(fd) to avoid race with fd reuse).

diagrams.title: Reactor Pattern: Architecture and Component Roles
diagrams.description: Classic Reactor pattern decomposition diagram. Components: (1) Event Demultiplexer — epoll_wait, returns (fd, event_type) pairs. (2) Dispatcher — iterates returned events, looks up registered handler for each fd+event combination. (3) Event Handlers — user callbacks, one per (fd, event_type) combination. (4) Timer Dispatcher — processes expired timer callbacks. (5) Deferred Queue — runs post-dispatch callbacks. Shows control flow: single-threaded main loop → demultiplexer → dispatcher → handlers → timer dispatch → deferred queue → repeat. Contrasts with Proactor: here completion is dispatch to read-ready callback, not to completion callback.

diagrams.title: reactor_t Struct Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Struct layout diagram for the reactor_t central data structure. Fields with byte offsets: [0] int epoll_fd (4B), [8] conn_state_t* conn_table (8B, array indexed by fd), [16] int conn_table_size (4B), [24] timer_heap_t timers (inline heap struct ~32B), [56] defer_queue_t deferred (inline queue ~24B), [80] pending_changes_t changes (array of epoll_ctl calls to defer). Shows which fields are hot (accessed every tick: epoll_fd, conn_table) vs cold (conn_table_size, only on accept). Cache line annotations for 64B boundaries.

diagrams.title: Event Dispatch Loop: Control Flow with Deferred Modifications
diagrams.description: Detailed flowchart of the reactor's main loop iteration. Step 1: timeout = timer_heap_next_expiry(). Step 2: n = epoll_wait(epoll_fd, events, MAX_EVENTS, timeout). Step 3: for i in 0..n → lookup conn_state by events[i].data.fd → if conn marked CLOSED → skip → else call registered callback(fd, event_flags). Step 4: apply_pending_changes() — process all deferred epoll_ctl ADD/MOD/DEL calls accumulated during Step 3. Step 5: timer_process_expired() — call all expired timer callbacks. Step 6: run_deferred_queue() — call all reactor_defer'd callbacks. Shows why steps 4, 5, 6 happen AFTER the I/O dispatch loop, not interleaved.

diagrams.title: Callback Re-entrancy: The Iterator Invalidation Problem
diagrams.description: Before/after diagram illustrating the re-entrancy bug. BUGGY path: epoll_wait returns [fd=5 EPOLLIN, fd=7 EPOLLIN] → dispatch fd=5 callback → callback calls reactor_deregister(fd=7) → immediately calls epoll_ctl DEL for fd=7 → dispatch fd=7 callback (fd=7 now closed/reused) → use-after-free or wrong handler. SAFE path: epoll_wait returns [fd=5 EPOLLIN, fd=7 EPOLLIN] → copy events to local array → dispatch fd=5 callback → callback calls reactor_deregister(fd=7) → adds to pending_changes queue, marks fd=7 CLOSED in conn_table → dispatch fd=7 callback → conn_table[7].state == CLOSED → SKIP → after loop: apply pending_changes → epoll_ctl DEL fd=7.

diagrams.title: Deferred Task Queue: Execution Order Within a Tick
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing one complete event loop tick with all phases labeled and ordered. Time axis: [epoll_wait returns] → [I/O callback 1] → [I/O callback 2 (queues deferred task A)] → [I/O callback 3] → [apply pending epoll_ctl changes] → [timer expired callback 1 (queues deferred task B)] → [timer expired callback 2] → [deferred task A runs] → [deferred task B runs] → [epoll_wait called again]. Annotates: 'deferred tasks always see all I/O and timer results from this tick, but run before next tick's I/O'. Maps to Node.js phases: I/O callbacks ≈ I/O phase, timer callbacks ≈ timers phase, deferred ≈ process.nextTick queue.

diagrams.title: EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR: Kernel-Reported Error Events
diagrams.description: Decision tree for event flag handling in the dispatcher. Input: events[i].events bitmask. Branch: if EPOLLERR set → dispatch with EVENT_ERROR flag (socket error, check getsockopt SO_ERROR). if EPOLLHUP set → dispatch with EVENT_HANGUP flag (peer closed, half-close or full close). if EPOLLRDHUP set → dispatch with EVENT_PEER_CLOSED flag (peer half-closed). if EPOLLIN set → dispatch with EVENT_READABLE. if EPOLLOUT set → dispatch with EVENT_WRITABLE. Note: EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR are ALWAYS reported even if not registered in events mask — must always handle in dispatcher.

diagrams.title: Per-Connection HTTP State Machine
diagrams.description: State diagram with all states and all transitions. States: READING_HEADERS → READING_BODY → PROCESSING → WRITING_RESPONSE → KEEP_ALIVE (loops to READING_HEADERS) → CLOSING → CLOSED. Transition triggers: READING_HEADERS: EPOLLIN → accumulate bytes → found \r\n\r\n → parse headers → if Content-Length > 0 → READING_BODY, else → PROCESSING. READING_BODY: EPOLLIN → accumulate bytes → received Content-Length bytes → PROCESSING. PROCESSING: synchronous handler → build response → WRITING_RESPONSE. WRITING_RESPONSE: write response → if EAGAIN → wait for EPOLLOUT → when buffer drained → if keep-alive → KEEP_ALIVE, else → CLOSING. Any state + timeout → CLOSING. Any state + EPOLLERR/EPOLLHUP → CLOSING.

diagrams.title: Incremental HTTP Parser: Accumulation Across Read Events
diagrams.description:
Data walk diagram showing a request split across three read() calls. Read 1 returns: 'GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: loc' (39 bytes) → appended to read_buf → parse attempt → no 

 found → stay in READING_HEADERS. Read 2 returns: 'alhost
Connection: keep-alive' (30 bytes) → appended → parse attempt → no 

 → stay. Read 3 returns: '

' (3 bytes) → appended → parse attempt → 

 found at offset 69 → parse method, path, version, headers → transition to PROCESSING. Shows read_buf as a growable byte array with len and capacity tracked separately. Annotates: max_header_size check to defend against header flooding.

diagrams.title: HTTP/1.1 Keep-Alive: State Reset and FD Reuse
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing a keep-alive connection serving three requests on a single TCP connection / single fd. Shows: request 1 received → response sent → state reset to READING_HEADERS → idle timer reset → request 2 received → response sent → state reset → request 3 received → Connection: close header → response sent → CLOSING state → connection torn down. Contrasts with HTTP/1.0: every request requires a new TCP connection (new fd, new accept, new handshake). Shows the file descriptor reuse benefit: 10K keep-alive clients hold 10K fds but can serve arbitrary request volume without fd exhaustion.

diagrams.title: Resource Cleanup on Connection Close: All Exit Paths
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing every path that leads to connection close and the mandatory cleanup sequence. Paths: (A) Idle timeout fires → timer callback → close_conn(fd). (B) read() returns 0 (EOF) → close_conn(fd). (C) read() returns -1 with errno != EAGAIN → close_conn(fd). (D) Write buffer exceeds max size → close_conn(fd). (E) HTTP Connection: close after response → close_conn(fd). (F) EPOLLERR/EPOLLHUP → close_conn(fd). All paths converge at close_conn() which must: (1) timer_cancel(conn_id), (2) epoll_ctl(DEL, fd) — BEFORE close(fd), (3) free(conn->read_buf), (4) free(conn->write_buf), (5) conn_table[fd] = NULL, (6) close(fd). Order is critical: epoll DEL must precede close to avoid fd reuse race.

diagrams.title: C10K Benchmark Architecture and Expected Results
diagrams.description: Benchmark setup diagram showing: wrk/ab client machine → network → server under test → event loop → 10K connection state machines. Shows wrk command: 'wrk -t12 -c10000 -d30s http://server:8080/index.html'. Expected results table: Connections: 10,000. Throughput: 50,000-100,000 req/sec. p50 latency: 1-5ms. p99 latency: <100ms. CPU: single core <80%. Memory: ~560KB for 10K connections (56 bytes/conn × 10K = 560KB conn state) + read/write buffers. Compares with Apache prefork: 10K connections = 10K processes × 8MB stack = 80GB RAM — infeasible. Annotates: use ulimit -n 65535 and net.core.somaxconn sysctl before benchmarking.

diagrams.title: Static File Serving Path with MIME Types
diagrams.description:
Data walk from HTTP GET request to response sent. Steps: parse path → sanitize (reject '../' path traversal) → build absolute path (server_root + path) → stat() to check existence and size → open() → read() file into response body → build headers: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: N

 → enqueue headers + body to write buffer → trigger write path (M2 write buffering). Shows MIME type lookup table: .html→text/html, .css→text/css, .js→application/javascript, .png→image/png. Annotates sendfile() as the production optimization skipped here for clarity.

diagrams.title: HTTP/1.1 Pipelining: Multiple In-Flight Requests
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing HTTP/1.1 pipelining. Client sends request 1 and request 2 back-to-back without waiting for response 1. Server read buffer receives both. State machine processes: parse request 1 from buffer → PROCESSING → generate response 1 → start writing → while writing, parse request 2 from remaining buffer → queue for processing. Shows that responses MUST be sent in order (response 1 before response 2) even if request 2 could be processed faster. Annotates: simple implementation uses a response queue per connection; failure to order causes client confusion. This is why HTTP/2 multiplexing was invented.

diagrams.title: Comparison: This Project vs. NGINX Worker Architecture
diagrams.description: Side-by-side architecture diagram. LEFT (this project): single process → single epoll instance → event loop → 10K conn state machines. RIGHT (NGINX): master process → N worker processes (one per CPU core) → each worker has epoll instance → EPOLLEXCLUSIVE or SO_REUSEPORT for accept balancing → each worker handles ~10K/N connections. Shows: NGINX uses the identical per-worker event loop you built; the only addition is multi-process scaling. Annotates NGINX's ngx_event_process_posted() as equivalent to your deferred task queue, and ngx_event_timer_rbtree as equivalent to your timer heap.

diagrams.title: Node.js libuv Event Loop: Mapping to This Project's Reactor
diagrams.description: Mapping diagram showing direct correspondence between libuv internals and the reactor you built. libuv phase → This project equivalent: 'timers (setTimeout/setInterval)' → timer_heap_t and timer_process_expired(). 'pending callbacks' → deferred close callbacks. 'I/O poll (uv__io_poll → epoll_wait)' → epoll_wait call with timeout. 'I/O callbacks' → registered callbacks in reactor_register(). 'setImmediate' → deferred_queue (reactor_defer()). 'process.nextTick' → immediate deferred queue (runs before setImmediate). Shows libuv's uv_run() as structurally identical to your reactor_run_once().

diagrams.title: Hardware Soul: Cache Line Analysis of Hot Path
diagrams.description: Cache line heat map for the event dispatch hot path. For each epoll_wait event: (1) events[i] — hot, array is sequential, prefetch-friendly, stays in L1 cache. (2) conn_table[fd] lookup — array indexed by fd number, random fd values = random cache misses → ~100ns L3 miss per connection. (3) conn->read_buf — cold pointer, another cache miss for the buffer itself. (4) read(fd, ...) syscall — ~1000ns kernel context switch. Annotates: at 100K events/sec, cache miss cost for conn_table dominates. Solution: keep conn_state_t small (fits in one cache line), sort events by fd to improve spatial locality. Compares: sequential socket buffers that fit in L2 vs random fd table access pattern.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A bottom-up construction of a production-grade single-threaded C10K server: raw epoll syscalls → write buffering and timer heap → reactor abstraction → HTTP/1.1 application layer. Each milestone is a vertical slice that compiles, runs, and is correct before the next layer is added. The reactor abstraction is the pivot point — below it, all epoll internals; above it, zero epoll knowledge. Hardware constraints (kernel socket buffers, cache lines, TLB pressure, syscall cost) drive every design decision.

modules.name: epoll Basics: Level-Triggered and Edge-Triggered
modules.description: Stands up the epoll instance, non-blocking sockets, per-fd connection table, and a complete echo server that demonstrates both LT and ET semantics. Does NOT implement write buffering, timers, or any application protocol.

specs.inputs: Raw TCP connections on a listening socket; incoming byte streams from clients
specs.outputs: Echo of received bytes back to client; per-fd conn_t entries tracking connection state
specs.abstractions: conn_t flat array indexed by fd; epoll interest set; kernel receive/send buffers
error_categories: EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK on read/write — not an error, resource temporarily unavailable
ECONNABORTED on accept4 — client reset before accept ran, safe to retry
EPOLLERR/EPOLLHUP — async error or peer hangup, close connection
EINTR on epoll_wait — signal interrupted syscall, retry
fd >= MAX_CONNS — fd table overflow, drop connection gracefully
ET drain failure — reading only once per event silently loses data (the primary pitfall)
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; all I/O multiplexed through one epoll instance; zero synchronization primitives required
performance_targets: epoll_wait latency: < 1μs when events are pending
accept4 throughput: drain entire backlog in a single loop iteration
conn_t lookup: O(1) array index, ~4 ns
ET drain loop: read until EAGAIN, ~1 syscall per 4KB of kernel receive buffer
Memory per connection: 4136 bytes (conn_t with 4KB read buffer)

implementation_phases.name: epoll instance creation and listening socket setup
Non-blocking accept loop with EAGAIN/ECONNABORTED handling
Per-fd conn_t array with conn_new/conn_free lifecycle
Level-triggered event loop with single-read-per-event echo
Edge-triggered event loop with drain-until-EAGAIN loop
Integration test: LT vs ET correctness with large payloads (> 4KB)

diagrams.title: epoll Kernel Data Structures: Interest Set (Red-Black Tree) vs Readiness Queue (Linked List)
diagrams.description: Two-pane diagram. Left pane: red-black tree of (fd, event_mask) pairs representing the interest set after epoll_ctl ADD calls for listen_fd and three client fds. Right pane: linked list of ready entries populated after a packet arrives on one client fd. Arrow from kernel packet-arrival interrupt handler pointing to the readiness queue insertion. Labels: O(log n) interest lookup, O(1) readiness dequeue. Annotation: 9950 idle connections in the interest set cost zero at epoll_wait time.

diagrams.title: Kernel Socket Buffer Model: Receive Buffer, Send Buffer, and EAGAIN Semantics
diagrams.description: Per-socket cross-section showing: NIC DMA → kernel receive buffer (annotated: default 87KB) ← read() copies to userspace. Userspace write buffer → kernel send buffer (annotated: default 16KB) → TCP stack → NIC. Three outcome rows for write() on non-blocking socket: (1) return > 0, == len: full success; (2) return > 0, < len: partial write, advance offset; (3) return -1, errno=EAGAIN: send buffer full, buffer remainder. Annotation: EAGAIN is not an error.

diagrams.title: conn_t Struct Memory Layout with Cache Line Boundaries
diagrams.description: Byte-offset table for conn_t: offset 0 state(4), offset 4 fd(4), offset 8 read_buf[4096], offset 4104 read_len(8). Total 4112 bytes = 64.25 cache lines. Color bands every 64 bytes showing cache line 0 (contains state+fd+first 56B of read_buf), cache lines 1–63 (read_buf body), cache line 64 (read_len). Annotation: accessing conn_t for the first time = ~64 cache miss loads from DRAM if cold.

diagrams.title: Per-FD Connection Table: Flat Array Indexed by File Descriptor Number
diagrams.description: Array of MAX_CONNS=10240 conn_t slots. Slots 0–3 (stdin/stdout/stderr/epfd) shown as reserved/gray. Slots 4 (listen_fd) and 5,6,7 (client fds) shown as active with state=CONN_ACTIVE. Slots 8–10239 shown as free (state=CONN_FREE, gray). Arrow from epoll event (data.fd=6) through array index to conn_table[6]. Annotation: O(1) lookup, no pointer chasing, cache-hostile under high load due to sparse access pattern.

diagrams.title: Level-Triggered vs Edge-Triggered: Kernel Notification State Machine
diagrams.description: Two parallel state machines side by side. LT machine: states [empty, has_data]. Transition 'data arrives' → has_data. From has_data: 'epoll_wait called' → reports fd as ready. 'partial read' → stays in has_data → reports fd as ready again on next epoll_wait. 'drain to EAGAIN' → transitions to empty. ET machine: states [empty, has_data]. Transition 'data arrives' → has_data AND adds to readiness queue ONCE. 'epoll_wait returns fd' → mark as delivered. No re-notification unless new data arrives (new empty→has_data transition). Red box: 'partial read in ET = data silently stranded'.

diagrams.title: ET Drain Loop: Correct vs Incorrect Control Flow
diagrams.description: Two flowcharts side by side. LEFT (WRONG): EPOLLIN event → read(fd, buf, 4096) → if n>0 echo and return. Annotation: 'if client sent 8192 bytes, 4096 remain in kernel buffer, no new EPOLLIN ever fires, connection hangs forever.' RIGHT (CORRECT): EPOLLIN event → loop: read(fd,buf,4096) → if n>0 echo, continue loop; if EAGAIN break loop; if n==0 or error close. Annotation: 'must drain to EAGAIN to guarantee no data is stranded.' Red path highlighting the wrong exit.

diagrams.title: Accept Loop Under High Load: Draining Multiple Queued Connections
diagrams.description: Sequence of events: time axis on left. T=0: 50 clients initiate TCP three-way handshakes. T=1ms: kernel completes all 50 handshakes, queues them in accept backlog. T=1ms: epoll_wait returns EPOLLIN on listen_fd. T=1ms+: accept4() called in tight loop iteration 1..50, each returning a new fd. Iteration 51: accept4() returns EAGAIN — loop exits. Annotation: LT mode would also drain the backlog across multiple epoll_wait cycles (re-notifies until EAGAIN), but the explicit loop is required in ET mode and is the correct pattern in both.

diagrams.title: epoll_ctl Lifecycle: ADD, MOD, DEL Ordering and the fd-Reuse Trap
diagrams.description: Timeline for a single connection. Step 1: accept4 → fd=7. Step 2: epoll_ctl(ADD, fd=7, EPOLLIN). Step 3: data events dispatched. Step 4: EOF detected. Step 5: epoll_ctl(DEL, fd=7, NULL) — MUST come before close. Step 6: close(fd=7) — OS recycles fd=7. Step 7: accept4 returns new connection as fd=7. Red warning box between steps 5 and 6: 'If DEL is skipped and OS recycles fd=7, a future DEL call removes the NEW connection from epoll interest set — silent bug, very hard to reproduce under low load.'

diagrams.title: Hardware Soul: Cache and Syscall Cost Analysis of the epoll Hot Path
diagrams.description: Annotated hot-path diagram for one epoll_wait cycle. Each operation labeled with cost: epoll_wait() [thread sleep → NIC interrupt → thread wake: 0 CPU cycles while sleeping]. kernel copy_to_user for events array [12KB sequential: L2 prefetch-friendly, ~12K cycles]. Dispatch loop iteration: conn_get(fd) [array index: L3 miss if cold = ~200 cycles, L1 hit if recent = ~4 cycles]. read() syscall [user→kernel ring transition: ~150 cycles + ~1 cycle/byte memcpy]. Branch prediction table: fd==listen_fd [rarely true, predictor 99% accurate], errno==EAGAIN [true once per drain, predictable after first iteration]. Total per-event hot-path estimate: 400–600 cycles for a warm L2 hit, 1000–2000 cycles for a cold L3 miss.

modules.name: Write Buffering and Timer Management
modules.description: Implements the write buffer with EPOLLOUT lifecycle management and a min-heap timer system integrated with epoll_wait timeout. Does NOT implement the reactor abstraction API or any protocol-level parsing.

specs.inputs: Partial write results (EAGAIN or short write) from write(); timer insertion/cancellation requests; epoll_wait return values indicating elapsed time
specs.outputs: Correctly ordered byte streams to clients with no data loss under backpressure; idle connection closure via timer expiry; epoll_wait timeout parameter derived from nearest timer
specs.abstractions: write_buf_t with offset-tracked flat buffer; timer_heap_t as min-heap with back-pointer from conn_t; conn_t extended with write buffer and timer_id fields; CLOCK_MONOTONIC time source via vDSO
error_categories: EAGAIN on write() — send buffer full, buffer remainder and arm EPOLLOUT
Partial write (0 < w < len) — advance offset, treat same as EAGAIN for remaining bytes
Write buffer overflow — client reading too slowly (slow loris); drop connection
EPOLLOUT busy loop — EPOLLOUT registered when write buffer is empty, causes 100% CPU
Timer heap corruption — heap_swap fails to update both heap_idx and conn_t->timer_id
Missing timer cancel on close — heap entry references recycled fd, fires on wrong connection
Multiple timer expirations missed — processing only first expired timer per tick
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; EPOLLOUT armed/disarmed via epoll_ctl(MOD); timers integrated with epoll_wait timeout parameter; CLOCK_MONOTONIC via vDSO (no syscall)
performance_targets: write() fast path (no backpressure): zero extra syscalls, zero buffer copies beyond direct write
EPOLLOUT arm/disarm: exactly two epoll_ctl(MOD) calls per backpressure event (arm on EAGAIN, disarm on drain)
now_ms() via vDSO: ~20 ns, called twice per epoll_wait iteration
Min-heap insert/cancel: O(log n), n=10000 → 13 comparisons × 24 bytes = ~312 bytes touched
Timer expiry scan: O(expired_count × log n), typically 0–5 expirations per tick
write_buf_t metadata: 32 bytes, fits in one cache line

implementation_phases.name: write_buf_t: flat buffer with offset tracking, append, consume, compact
conn_write() with partial write detection and EAGAIN handling
EPOLLOUT arm/disarm lifecycle (epoll_ctl MOD) and flush loop
Min-heap implementation: insert, sift-up, sift-down, swap with back-pointer updates
Timer cancel, reset, and timer_expire_all() with while loop
epoll_wait timeout integration: compute_epoll_timeout() and wire into event loop
Full connection close path: cancel timer, DEL epoll, free write_buf, close fd

diagrams.title: write_buf_t Struct Memory Layout and Offset Mechanics
diagrams.description: Top section: byte-offset table for write_buf_t: offset 0 data(8 ptr), offset 8 capacity(8), offset 16 write_offset(8), offset 24 write_len(8). Total 32 bytes = exactly half a cache line. Bottom section: three-state diagram of the data[] heap allocation: (A) Initial: offset=0, len=0, capacity=65536. (B) After append of 3000 bytes: offset=0, len=3000. (C) After partial write of 2000 bytes: offset=2000, len=3000, pending=1000. (D) After compaction (offset > capacity/2): memmove pending bytes to front, offset=0, len=1000. Arrow annotated: 'compaction cost = O(pending bytes), amortized O(1) per byte appended'.

diagrams.title: Write Path Decision Tree: Direct Write vs Buffer vs EPOLLOUT
diagrams.description: Flowchart starting at 'conn_write(fd, src, len) called'. Branch 1: write_buf_pending > 0 → append to buffer (must preserve ordering), return. Branch 2: write_buf_pending == 0 → attempt direct write() in loop. Sub-branches: write() returns len → success, EPOLLOUT stays disarmed, done. write() returns 0 < w < len → advance sent, continue loop. write() returns EAGAIN → append remainder to buffer, call conn_arm_epollout(), return 0. write() returns error (not EAGAIN) → return -1, caller closes connection. conn_arm_epollout box: if already armed (epollout_armed==1) return immediately; else epoll_ctl(MOD, EPOLLIN|EPOLLOUT), set epollout_armed=1.

diagrams.title: EPOLLOUT Busy Loop Anti-Pattern vs Correct Lifecycle
diagrams.description: Two timeline diagrams. TOP (WRONG): EPOLLOUT registered at connection creation. epoll_wait → returns fd with EPOLLOUT. flush(): write_buf empty, nothing to do, return. epoll_wait → returns fd with EPOLLOUT again immediately. Loop continues at ~10M iterations/sec consuming 100% CPU. perf annotation: 'dTLB-load-misses: 0, but instructions retired: maxed'. BOTTOM (CORRECT): EPOLLOUT disarmed at connection creation (only EPOLLIN). On EAGAIN from write(): arm EPOLLOUT via MOD. On EPOLLOUT event: flush buffer. If buffer drained: disarm EPOLLOUT via MOD. epoll_wait now blocks until new data arrives. Annotation: 'exactly 2 epoll_ctl(MOD) calls per backpressure event.'

diagrams.title: Min-Heap Timer Structure: Array Representation and Parent-Child Relationships
diagrams.description: Two-part diagram. LEFT: visual binary tree with 7 nodes showing expiry times in ms: root=1000ms (idx 0), children: 1500ms (idx 1), 1200ms (idx 2), grandchildren: 2000ms (idx 3), 1600ms (idx 4), 1800ms (idx 5), 3000ms (idx 6). RIGHT: flat array[7] showing same values with parent formula parent=(i-1)/2 and child formulas left=2i+1, right=2i+2 annotated. Each array cell shows: expiry_ms, fd, heap_idx. Arrow from conn_table[fd].timer_id back to the array index. Invariant box: 'every parent expiry_ms ≤ both children expiry_ms; root is always the nearest expiry'.

diagrams.title: Heap Sift-Up After Insert: Step-by-Step State Transitions
diagrams.description: Four frames showing a heap insert of a new timer with expiry=900ms. Frame 1: heap before insert, root=1000ms. Frame 2: new entry appended at index 7 (expiry=900ms), heap_idx=7, conn_t->timer_id=7. Frame 3: sift-up iteration 1 — compare with parent at (7-1)/2=3 (expiry=2000ms): 900 < 2000, swap. heap_swap updates heap_idx in both entries AND conn_t->timer_id for both fds. Frame 4: sift-up iteration 2 — compare with parent at (3-1)/2=1 (expiry=1500ms): 900 < 1500, swap. Frame 5: compare with root (expiry=1000ms): 900 < 1000, swap. New root is 900ms. Final state: root=900ms, previous root demoted. Annotation: 'log2(10000)≈13 iterations max; sift terminates as soon as parent ≤ child'.

diagrams.title: Timer Cancel: Swap-With-Last Pattern and Dual Sift to Restore Heap Order
diagrams.description: Three frames. Frame 1: heap with 6 entries; target to cancel is at heap_idx=2 (expiry=1200ms). Frame 2: swap entry at idx=2 with last entry at idx=5 (expiry=1800ms). heap_size-- (now 5). Update heap_idx in both timer_entry_t AND conn_t->timer_id for both fds. Frame 3a: sift-up from idx=2 — parent at idx=0 has expiry=1000ms ≤ 1800ms: no swap, sift-up terminates immediately. Frame 3b: sift-down from idx=2 — left child at idx=5 (expiry=3000ms) > 1800ms, right child at idx=6 doesn't exist: no swap, sift-down terminates. Final: valid heap with 5 entries. Annotation: 'calling both sift-up and sift-down handles all cases: only one performs swaps'.

diagrams.title: epoll_wait Timeout Integration: Computing Next Timer Expiry
diagrams.description: Flowchart for compute_epoll_timeout(). Input: timer_heap with heap_size entries. Branch: heap_size == 0 → return -1 (block indefinitely). Branch: heap_size > 0 → get root expiry_ms. get now_ms() via CLOCK_MONOTONIC vDSO. Compute diff = expiry - now. Branch: diff ≤ 0 → return 0 (already overdue, poll immediately). Branch: diff > INT_MAX → return INT_MAX (cap). Else: return (int)diff. Timeline diagram below: three epoll_wait calls shown. Call 1: timeout=5000ms (next timer 5s away), blocks for 4800ms until client data arrives. Call 2: timeout=200ms (timer nearly due), blocks 200ms, returns nready=0 (pure timeout). Call 3: timer_expire_all() runs, closes idle connection.

diagrams.title: Full Connection Lifecycle with Write Buffer and Timer: Create → Active → Timeout/Close
diagrams.description: State machine with 5 states: ACCEPT → ACTIVE (EPOLLIN watching) → BACKPRESSURE (EPOLLIN|EPOLLOUT watching) → DRAINING (returning to ACTIVE after flush) → CLOSED. Parallel timer lifecycle shown as a swimlane below: on ACCEPT: timer_insert(now+30s). On EPOLLIN data: timer_cancel + timer_insert(now+30s) (reset). On EPOLLOUT drain complete: no timer change. On timer expiry: close conn (cancel both timer and epoll). On explicit close: timer_cancel → epoll_ctl DEL → write_buf_free → close(fd). Each transition labeled with the exact function call sequence. Red annotations on the CLOSED state: 'must execute all 4 cleanup steps in order; missing any = resource leak'.

modules.name: Reactor API and Callback Dispatch
modules.description: Wraps all epoll internals behind a clean reactor_t API with I/O registration, one-shot and repeating timers, deferred task scheduling, and re-entrancy-safe dispatch. Does NOT implement any application protocol. After this milestone, user code never calls epoll_ctl or epoll_wait directly.

specs.inputs: reactor_register(fd, events, cb, udata) calls; reactor_set_timeout/interval calls; reactor_defer calls; raw epoll_wait events from kernel
specs.outputs: Callback invocations with correct event flags; timer callback firings at correct wall-clock times; deferred callbacks after I/O dispatch phase; safe operation when callbacks mutate registration state
specs.abstractions: reactor_t opaque struct; fd_handler_t registration table; timer_entry_t pool with min-heap; deferred ring buffer; pending_mod_t queue for re-entrancy deferral; REACTOR_READ/WRITE/ERROR flag abstraction over EPOLLIN/OUT/ERR/HUP
error_categories: Callback calls reactor_deregister on own fd during dispatch — must mark closing=1, defer epoll_ctl DEL
Callback calls reactor_register on new fd during dispatch — must defer epoll_ctl ADD
Deferred task re-enqueues itself — snapshot deferred_tail before draining to bound current-tick execution
Timer fires on a connection fd that was closed within same tick — closing guard prevents double-free
EPOLLHUP/EPOLLERR delivered without explicit subscription — always translate to REACTOR_ERROR
reactor_cancel_timer on already-fired one-shot timer — linear scan returns -1 safely
pending_ops queue overflow (> MAX_PENDING_OPS modifications per tick) — return -1 from register/deregister
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; dispatching flag serializes epoll_ctl calls relative to dispatch loop; pending_ops is a single-producer single-consumer queue (trivially lock-free in single-threaded context); deferred ring buffer uses head/tail snapshot for bounded execution
performance_targets: I/O callback dispatch overhead: one fd_handler_t lookup (array index) + one indirect call through function pointer = ~5–10 ns on warm L1
Pending ops application (Phase 2): MAX_PENDING_OPS=256 × 12 bytes = 3KB, fits in L1
Deferred queue drain (Phase 3): MAX_DEFERRED=1024 × 16 bytes = 16KB, fits in L2
Timer expiry scan: O(k log n) where k = expired timers this tick, typically 0–3
reactor_cancel_timer linear scan: O(max_timers), acceptable at 10K with ~130μs max cost
reactor_t total fixed footprint: ~19.5 KB

implementation_phases.name: reactor_t struct definition and reactor_create/destroy
reactor_register and reactor_deregister with dispatching guard and pending_ops queue
Four-phase dispatch loop: I/O dispatch → apply pending ops → deferred tasks → expire timers
reactor_set_timeout, reactor_set_interval, reactor_cancel_timer with timer pool indirection
reactor_defer with ring buffer and snapshot-based bounded drain
Echo server rewritten against reactor API with zero epoll symbols in user code

diagrams.title: reactor_t Internal Struct Layout: All Fields with Byte Offsets and Embedded Arrays
diagrams.description: Byte-offset table for reactor_t. Section 1 (scalars): epfd(4), running(4), dispatching(4), pad(4), max_fds(4), timer_pool_size(4), timer_heap_size(4), next_timer_id(4), pending_count(4), deferred_head(4), deferred_tail(4), pad(4). Section 2 (pointers): handlers ptr(8), timer_pool ptr(8), timer_heap ptr(8). Section 3 (embedded arrays): deferred[1024] at offset X (1024 × 16 = 16384 bytes); pending_ops[256] at offset X+16384 (256 × 12 = 3072 bytes). Total: ~19.5 KB. Cache line bands overlaid. Annotation: 'deferred[] and pending_ops[] embedded to avoid pointer chasing in hot path; heap-allocated handlers/timer_pool to support runtime-configured max_fds'.

diagrams.title: Reactor Pattern Component Map: Demultiplexer, Dispatcher, Handler Table, Concrete Handlers
diagrams.description: Four-box architecture diagram. Box 1 (Event Demultiplexer): epoll_wait() — waits on kernel events, returns array of epoll_events. Box 2 (Dispatcher): the for-loop over events[0..nready-1] — routes each event to the correct handler via handlers[fd].callback. Box 3 (Handler Registration Table): fd_handler_t handlers[] array — maps fd → {callback, udata, events, registered, closing}. Box 4 (Concrete Handlers): accept_handler, echo_handler, http_io_callback — user-provided, zero epoll knowledge. Arrows: kernel→Demultiplexer (events), Demultiplexer→Dispatcher (nready events), Dispatcher→Handler Table (fd lookup), Handler Table→Concrete Handler (callback(fd, rev, udata)). Boundary line between 'reactor internals' (boxes 1–3) and 'user code' (box 4).

diagrams.title: Callback Re-entrancy: The Iterator Invalidation Bug and the Mark-and-Skip Fix
diagrams.description: Two sequence diagrams. LEFT (BUG): epoll_wait returns events=[fd=5 EPOLLIN, fd=5 EPOLLIN]. i=0: callback called for fd=5. Callback calls reactor_deregister(fd=5) → immediately calls epoll_ctl(DEL) and close(fd=5). OS recycles fd=5 for new connection. i=1: handlers[5] now points to new connection's handler or is zeroed. Callback invoked on wrong connection. Result: undefined behavior / double-free / wrong connection closed. RIGHT (FIX): Callback calls reactor_deregister(fd=5) → dispatching==1 → sets h->closing=1, enqueues PENDING_DEL. Return to dispatch loop. i=1: check h->closing==1 → skip event. Phase 2: apply PENDING_DEL, clear handlers[5]. No use-after-close.

diagrams.title: Four-Phase Dispatch Loop: Control Flow, Phase Boundaries, and dispatching Flag Lifecycle
diagrams.description: Vertical flowchart of one reactor_run iteration. Pre-loop: compute timeout from timer heap. Call epoll_wait(). On EINTR: expire timers, retry. PHASE 1 box: set dispatching=1. for i in 0..nready: lookup handlers[fd], skip if !registered||closing, translate epoll flags to REACTOR_* flags, call h->callback(). After loop: set dispatching=0. PHASE 2 box: for each pending_op: if PENDING_DEL call epoll_ctl(DEL) and memset handler slot; if PENDING_ADD/MOD call epoll_ctl(ADD/MOD). Reset pending_count=0. PHASE 3 box: snapshot drain_until=deferred_tail. while deferred_head!=drain_until: pop task, call callback, advance head. PHASE 4 box: call timer_expire_all(). Arrows showing that callbacks in PHASE 1 enqueue to pending_ops (PHASE 2 input) and deferred queue (PHASE 3 input). Annotation: 'dispatching=0 must be set BEFORE Phase 2 so that any callbacks within deferred tasks see dispatching=0 and execute epoll_ctl immediately'.

diagrams.title: Deferred Task Queue: Ring Buffer Layout and Snapshot-Based Bounded Drain
diagrams.description: Ring buffer diagram with 1024 slots. deferred_head and deferred_tail pointers shown. Scenario A (normal): 3 tasks enqueued (tail=3, head=0). Phase 3: snapshot drain_until=3. Drain tasks 0,1,2. Head advances to 3 = drain_until, loop exits. Scenario B (re-entrant defer): 2 tasks enqueued (tail=2, head=0). Phase 3: snapshot drain_until=2. Processing task 0: task calls reactor_defer(), tail advances to 3. Processing task 1. Head=2=drain_until, loop exits. New task at slot 2 is left for next tick. Annotation: 'without snapshot, re-entrant defer creates infinite loop in same tick'. Buffer-full detection: (tail+1)%MAX == head → return -1.

diagrams.title: reactor_register During Dispatch: Immediate vs Deferred epoll_ctl Path
diagrams.description: Decision tree for reactor_register(r, fd, events, cb, udata). Branch 1: r->dispatching == 0 (outside dispatch). Sub-branch A: fd not previously registered → epoll_ctl(ADD), set handlers[fd]. Sub-branch B: fd already registered → epoll_ctl(MOD), update handlers[fd]. Branch 2: r->dispatching == 1 (inside dispatch callback). Sub-branch A: fd not registered → update handlers[fd] immediately (safe, only modifies our array, not epoll state), enqueue PENDING_ADD to pending_ops. Sub-branch B: fd registered → update handlers[fd], enqueue PENDING_MOD. In both dispatch sub-branches: the handler table is updated immediately so subsequent callback lookups see the new callback/udata; only the epoll_ctl call is deferred. Annotation: 'handler table update is safe because the dispatch loop only reads it at the top of each iteration, not inside a callback'.

diagrams.title: One-Shot vs Repeating Timer: Heap State Before and After Expiry
diagrams.description: Two scenarios. SCENARIO A (one-shot, interval_ms=0): timer at root expires. Capture timer_id, cb, udata. Swap root with last entry, heap_size--. sift_down from root. memset expired entry to 0. Call cb(tid, udata). Result: timer is gone from heap; cb may call reactor_cancel_timer(tid) and get -1 (already removed, safe). SCENARIO B (repeating, interval_ms=5000): timer at root expires. Capture values. Update expiry_ms = now + interval_ms. sift_down from root (do NOT remove from heap). Call cb(tid, udata). If cb calls reactor_cancel_timer(tid): timer is still in heap, cancel finds it and removes it correctly. Key ordering annotation: 'reschedule BEFORE calling cb; if rescheduled after and cb cancels, cancel removes the old entry then reschedule adds a ghost entry with same timer_id'.

diagrams.title: EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR: Kernel-Reported Events and Translation to REACTOR_ERROR
diagrams.description: Three-column table. Column 1: kernel event flag. Column 2: when kernel generates it. Column 3: reactor translation and recommended handler action. Row 1: EPOLLERR — async socket error (RST received, connect failed) — REACTOR_ERROR, getsockopt(SO_ERROR) to retrieve errno, close connection. Row 2: EPOLLHUP — peer closed write side (TCP FIN received), remote half-close — REACTOR_ERROR, read any remaining buffered data then close. Row 3: EPOLLRDHUP — specific half-close signal (Linux 2.6.17+) — REACTOR_ERROR, stop reading, begin close. Row 4: EPOLLIN|EPOLLERR simultaneously — data available AND error — REACTOR_READ|REACTOR_ERROR, handle error first, discard data. Annotation: 'EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR are delivered regardless of subscription; cannot opt out; always include REACTOR_ERROR check before REACTOR_READ in callback'.

modules.name: HTTP Server on Event Loop
modules.description: Builds a complete HTTP/1.1 server on top of the M3 reactor API: incremental request parser, six-state per-connection state machine, keep-alive with pipelining, static file serving with MIME types, two-tier timeout protection, and a benchmarked C10K demonstration. Does NOT implement HTTP/2, TLS, or multi-worker architecture.

specs.inputs: Raw TCP byte streams from HTTP/1.1 clients; file system (static serve root); benchmark traffic from wrk/ab at 10K concurrent connections
specs.outputs: HTTP/1.1 responses with correct status codes, Content-Type, Content-Length, and Connection headers; static file bodies; 400/404/405/408/413 error responses; benchmark metrics (req/sec, p99 latency)
specs.abstractions: http_conn_t per-connection struct with read buffer, write buffer (M2), state machine, two timer IDs; http_request_t parsed request; incremental parser returning PARSE_INCOMPLETE/COMPLETE/ERROR/TOO_LARGE; six-state FSM (READING_HEADERS→READING_BODY→PROCESSING→WRITING_RESPONSE→KEEP_ALIVE→CLOSING)
error_categories: PARSE_INCOMPLETE — headers not yet complete, return and wait for more EPOLLIN data
PARSE_ERROR — malformed request line or headers, send 400 and close
PARSE_TOO_LARGE — read buffer full before \r\n\r\n found, send 413 and close
Path traversal attempt — resolve_path() finds realpath escapes serve_root, send 404
File not found or not regular file — open()/fstat() fails, send 404
Method not allowed — non-GET/HEAD, send 405
Write buffer overflow — client reading too slowly, close connection (slow loris defense)
Header timeout (10s) — incomplete headers, send 408 and close
Idle timeout (30s) — no data received, close connection
Double-close from timer + REACTOR_ERROR in same tick — state==CLOSING guard prevents second free
Pipelined request bytes left in read_buf after response — must defer re-parse to Phase 3
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded reactor; all HTTP state mutations happen exclusively in reactor callbacks (Phase 1) and deferred tasks (Phase 3); no shared mutable state between connection handlers; two independent timer IDs per connection (idle + header deadline)
performance_targets: C10K: 10,000 concurrent connections on a single thread with p99 latency < 100ms
Throughput: 100K+ req/sec for small static files (index.html) on loopback
Memory: ~26KB per http_conn_t; 10K connections = ~260MB heap
Parser throughput: find_header_end() O(n) scan, ~1 ns/byte sequential, ~4μs for 4KB headers
File serve latency for hot files: < 1ms (page cache hit: inode lookup ~3000 cycles + read memcpy ~100ns/KB)
Connection accept rate: drain full kernel backlog (1024) in < 1ms via accept4 loop
Timer operations per tick under load: ~400 cancel+insert (200 connections × 2 resets); ~5200 heap comparisons total

implementation_phases.name: http_conn_t struct and connection lifecycle (create on accept, close on all exit paths)
find_header_end() and parse_request_line() for request line parsing
parse_headers() with Content-Length and Connection header extraction
http_try_parse() top-level incremental parser integrating all parse stages
http_handle_read() drain loop with state transitions and parse-on-each-chunk
resolve_path() with realpath() path traversal prevention and MIME type table
http_process_request(): file open/stat/read and response header construction
http_handle_write() with EPOLLOUT lifecycle and keep-alive state reset
Pipelining: deferred re-parse via reactor_defer after response sent
Two-tier timeout (idle 30s + header deadline 10s) with http_send_error 408
System tuning (ulimit, somaxconn, ip_local_port_range) and wrk/ab benchmark

diagrams.title: http_conn_t Struct Memory Layout with Cache Line Boundaries
