### Phase 11 — System tuning and C10K benchmark (1–1.5 hours)
Create the complete `http_server.c` with accept handler, system tuning in `main()`, and benchmark infrastructure.
```c
/* http_server.c — main entry point */
#include "http_conn.h"
#include "reactor.h"
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <sys/resource.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <errno.h>
char g_serve_root[HTTP_SERVE_ROOT_MAX];
static reactor_t *g_reactor;
static void http_accept_cb(int listen_fd, uint32_t events, void *udata) {
    (void)udata;
    if (events & REACTOR_ERROR) return;
    while (1) {
        int fd = accept4(listen_fd, NULL, NULL, SOCK_NONBLOCK | SOCK_CLOEXEC);
        if (fd == -1) {
            if (errno == EAGAIN || errno == EWOULDBLOCK) break;
            if (errno == ECONNABORTED) continue;
            if (errno == EMFILE || errno == ENFILE) {
                fprintf(stderr, "accept4: fd table exhausted\n"); break;
            }
            perror("accept4"); break;
        }
        http_conn_t *conn = http_conn_new(fd, g_reactor);
        if (!conn) { close(fd); continue; }
        conn->idle_timer_id = reactor_set_timeout(g_reactor,
                                  HTTP_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS, http_idle_timeout_cb, conn);
        conn->header_timer_id = reactor_set_timeout(g_reactor,
                                    HTTP_HEADER_TIMEOUT_MS, http_header_timeout_cb, conn);
        if (reactor_register(g_reactor, fd, REACTOR_READ, http_io_callback, conn) != 0) {
            http_conn_close(conn);
        }
    }
}
static void tune_system(void) {
    /* Increase fd limit to maximum allowed */
    struct rlimit rl;
    getrlimit(RLIMIT_NOFILE, &rl);
    rl.rlim_cur = rl.rlim_max;
    setrlimit(RLIMIT_NOFILE, &rl);
    fprintf(stderr, "fd limit: %lu\n", (unsigned long)rl.rlim_cur);
}
int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    if (argc < 3) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s <port> <serve_root>\n", argv[0]);
        return 1;
    }
    int port = atoi(argv[1]);
    snprintf(g_serve_root, sizeof(g_serve_root), "%s", argv[2]);
    /* Strip trailing slash */
    size_t rlen = strlen(g_serve_root);
    if (rlen > 1 && g_serve_root[rlen - 1] == '/') g_serve_root[rlen - 1] = '\0';
    tune_system();
    g_reactor = reactor_create(65536, 65536);
    if (!g_reactor) { perror("reactor_create"); return 1; }
    int listen_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM | SOCK_NONBLOCK | SOCK_CLOEXEC, 0);
    if (listen_fd == -1) { perror("socket"); return 1; }
    int reuse = 1;
    setsockopt(listen_fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, &reuse, sizeof(reuse));
    struct sockaddr_in addr = {
        .sin_family      = AF_INET,
        .sin_port        = htons(port),
        .sin_addr.s_addr = INADDR_ANY,
    };
    if (bind(listen_fd, (struct sockaddr *)&addr, sizeof(addr)) == -1) {
        perror("bind"); return 1;
    }
    if (listen(listen_fd, 1024) == -1) { perror("listen"); return 1; }
    reactor_register(g_reactor, listen_fd, REACTOR_READ, http_accept_cb, NULL);
    printf("http_server on :%d serving %s\n", port, g_serve_root);
    reactor_run(g_reactor);
    reactor_destroy(g_reactor);
    close(listen_fd);
    return 0;
}
```
**Benchmark procedure** (in `test_http.sh`):
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
# System tuning
ulimit -n 65536
echo 65536 | sudo tee /proc/sys/fs/file-max > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
echo 65536 | sudo tee /proc/sys/net/core/somaxconn > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
echo "1024 65535" | sudo tee /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
# Prepare serve root
mkdir -p /tmp/bench_root
echo '<html><body>Hello C10K</body></html>' > /tmp/bench_root/index.html
# Start server
./http_server 8080 /tmp/bench_root &
SERVER_PID=$!
sleep 0.3
echo "=== Warmup (30s, 100 connections) ==="
wrk -t4 -c100 -d30s http://127.0.0.1:8080/index.html
echo "=== C10K Benchmark (60s, 10000 connections, --latency) ==="
wrk -t12 -c10000 -d60s --latency http://127.0.0.1:8080/index.html | tee /tmp/wrk_results.txt
# Parse and assert p99 < 100ms
P99=$(grep "99%" /tmp/wrk_results.txt | awk '{print $2}')
echo "p99 latency: $P99"
# wrk reports in ms; extract numeric part
P99_MS=$(echo "$P99" | sed 's/ms//')
python3 -c "
import sys
p99 = float('$P99_MS')
print(f'p99 = {p99:.2f}ms')
if p99 < 100.0:
    print('PASS: p99 < 100ms')
else:
    print('FAIL: p99 >= 100ms')
    sys.exit(1)
"
kill $SERVER_PID
```

## 8. Test Specification
### 8.1 `find_header_end`
| Test | Input | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| Minimal GET | `"GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n"` | Points past final `\r\n` |
| Incomplete (no `\r\n\r\n`) | `"GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: x"` | NULL |
| Exactly 4 bytes | `"\r\n\r\n"` | Points to byte 4 |
| Partial `\r\n\r` | `"abc\r\n\r"` | NULL |
| `\r\n\r\n` mid-string | `"abc\r\n\r\ndef"` | Points to `"def"` |
| Empty buffer | `len == 0` | NULL |
| `len == 3` | Any 3 bytes | NULL |
### 8.2 `parse_request_line`
| Test | Input | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| Minimal GET | `"GET / HTTP/1.1"` | PARSE_COMPLETE; method="GET", path="/", minor=1 |
| HTTP/1.0 | `"HEAD /robots.txt HTTP/1.0"` | PARSE_COMPLETE; minor=0 |
| Path with query | `"GET /path?a=b HTTP/1.1"` | PARSE_COMPLETE; path="/path?a=b" |
| No spaces | `"GETHTTP/1.1"` | PARSE_ERROR |
| One space only | `"GET/HTTP/1.1"` | PARSE_ERROR |
| Unknown version | `"GET / HTTP/2.0"` | PARSE_ERROR |
| Empty method | `" / HTTP/1.1"` | PARSE_ERROR |
| Empty path | `"GET  HTTP/1.1"` (double space) | PARSE_ERROR (path_len == 0) |
| Path too long | Path of length `HTTP_MAX_PATH_LEN` | PARSE_TOO_LARGE |
### 8.3 `parse_headers`
| Test | Setup | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| Content-Length extraction | `Content-Length: 42` | `req.content_length == 42` |
| Connection: close | `Connection: close` on HTTP/1.1 | `req.keep_alive == 0` |
| Connection: keep-alive | `Connection: keep-alive` on HTTP/1.0 | `req.keep_alive == 1` |
| HTTP/1.1 default keep-alive | No Connection header, HTTP/1.1 | `req.keep_alive == 1` |
| HTTP/1.0 default close | No Connection header, HTTP/1.0 | `req.keep_alive == 0` |
| Case-insensitive header names | `CONTENT-LENGTH: 10` | `req.content_length == 10` |
| Whitespace trimming | `X-Foo:  value  ` (trimmed) | Value stored without leading space |
| Malformed line (no colon) | `NoColonHeader` | Line skipped; PARSE_COMPLETE |
| Header count limit | 33 headers | 32 stored; 33rd silently dropped |
| header_buf overflow | Sum of names+values > 8192 | PARSE_TOO_LARGE |
| Empty headers section | Only `\r\n\r\n` | PARSE_COMPLETE; header_count == 0 |
### 8.4 `http_try_parse`
| Test | Setup | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| Complete single chunk | Full GET in one buffer | PARSE_COMPLETE; state = PROCESSING |
| Split across 3 chunks | Chunk1, chunk2, chunk3 each incomplete | INCOMPLETE, INCOMPLETE, COMPLETE |
| Buffer full, no terminator | 16384 bytes, no `\r\n\r\n` | PARSE_TOO_LARGE |
| Request with body, partial | `Content-Length: 100`, body 50 bytes so far | PARSE_INCOMPLETE |
| Request with body, complete | `Content-Length: 5`, body "hello" | PARSE_COMPLETE |
| Pipelined: two requests | Two full GETs concatenated | First completes; second bytes in read_buf |
| Called in wrong state | `conn->state == PROCESSING` | PARSE_ERROR |
### 8.5 `resolve_path`
| Test | serve_root | req_path | Expected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal file | `/tmp/srv` | `/index.html` | 0; out = `/tmp/srv/index.html` |
| Traversal `..` | `/tmp/srv` | `/../etc/passwd` | -1 |
| Double traversal | `/tmp/srv` | `/sub/../../etc/shadow` | -1 |
| Directory path | `/tmp/srv` | `/` | 0; out ends with `/index.html` |
| Prefix match trap | `/tmp/srv` | `/../srv2/secret` | -1 (boundary check) |
| Nonexistent file | `/tmp/srv` | `/missing.html` | -1 |
| Symlink outside | Symlink in `/tmp/srv` pointing to `/etc` | -1 |
### 8.6 `http_process_request`
| Test | Setup | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| GET existing file | `req.method="GET"`, valid path | 200 OK; correct Content-Length |
| HEAD request | `req.method="HEAD"` | 200 OK; no body; correct Content-Length header |
| POST request | `req.method="POST"` | 405 response |
| DELETE request | `req.method="DELETE"` | 405 response |
| Missing file | Path does not exist | 404 response |
| Traversal attempt | Path resolves outside root | 404 response |
| Directory without index | Directory exists, no `index.html` | 404 response |
| Correct MIME type | `.html` file | `Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8` |
| Correct MIME type | `.png` file | `Content-Type: image/png` |
| Unknown extension | `.xyz` file | `Content-Type: application/octet-stream` |
| Large file (> write_buf) | File > 64KB | All bytes served; EPOLLOUT triggered |
### 8.7 Keep-Alive and Pipelining
| Test | Setup | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP/1.1 keep-alive | One connection, two sequential requests | Both responses received; connection stays open |
| HTTP/1.0 close | `Connection: close` explicit | Connection closed after first response |
| Pipelined 2 requests | Two GETs in one TCP write | Two 200 responses received |
| Pipelined 3 requests | Three GETs in one TCP write | Three 200 responses received |
| Keep-alive idle timeout | Response sent, no further requests for 31s | Connection closed at ~30s |
| State reset on keep-alive | After first response: `read_len == pipelined_bytes_or_0`, `request` zeroed | State machine clean for next request |
### 8.8 Timeout Behavior
| Test | Setup | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| Idle timeout fires | Connect, send nothing, wait | Connection closed at ~30s ± 1s |
| Idle resets on data | Send byte at 29s | Timer resets; connection survives to ~59s |
| Header timeout fires | Send incomplete headers over 12s | 408 response at ~10s |
| Header timeout cancelled | Headers complete before 10s | No spurious 408 |
| Double-close prevention | Timer fires same tick as REACTOR_ERROR | Only one `http_conn_close` executes |
### 8.9 Resource Cleanup
| Test | Setup | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| Normal close | Client sends FIN | write_buf freed, timer cancelled, fd closed |
| Error close | REACTOR_ERROR | Same cleanup path |
| Timeout close | Timer fires | Same cleanup path |
| Valgrind: 1000 connections | 1000 connections, all close normally | 0 bytes lost |
| fd leak check | 1000 connections with valgrind --track-fds | 0 extra open fds at exit |
---
## 9. Performance Targets
| Operation | Target | How to Measure |
|---|---|---|
| C10K: 10,000 concurrent connections | Server accepts all within 2s | `wrk -c10000 -d60s`; observe accept rate at start |
| Throughput (small file, loopback) | ≥ 100K req/sec | `wrk -t12 -c10000 -d60s --latency` |
| p99 latency at 10K connections | < 100ms | wrk `--latency` output, 99th percentile row |
| p50 latency at 10K connections | < 5ms | wrk `--latency` output, 50th percentile row |
| Memory per connection | ≤ 30KB (conn struct + write_buf) | `valgrind massif`; `26KB conn + 64KB write_buf heap` |
| Total heap at 10K connections | ≤ 920MB (26KB + 64KB) × 10K | `ps -o rss` or `/proc/pid/status VmRSS` |
| `find_header_end` for 4KB headers | < 5 µs | `clock_gettime` around 100K calls in tight loop |
| `http_try_parse` for complete request | < 10 µs | `clock_gettime` around 100K calls |
| `http_process_request` for hot file | < 500 µs | `clock_gettime` when file is in page cache |
| `http_conn_new` + `write_buf_new` | < 5 µs | `clock_gettime` around 10K allocations |
| `http_conn_close` (all cleanup) | < 3 µs | `clock_gettime` around 10K closes |
| Timer operations per tick (200 active conns) | < 200 µs total | `perf stat` on `reactor_cancel_timer` + `reactor_set_timeout` |
| Server CPU at 10K idle connections | < 0.1% | `pidstat -u 1` for 60s during idle |
| `grep -c epoll http_*.c` | 0 | Automated check in `test_http.sh` |
| Compile: `-O2 -Wall -Wextra -Werror` | Zero warnings | Mandatory; CI-enforced |
**Compile command:**
```bash
gcc -O2 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -g \
    -o http_server \
    http_server.c http_conn.c http_handlers.c http_process.c \
    http_parser.c http_path.c http_mime.c \
    reactor_create.c reactor_io.c reactor_timer.c \
    reactor_defer.c reactor_run.c \
    write_buf.c
```

## 10. State Machine
### HTTP Connection State Machine
```
States:
  READING_HEADERS  — accumulating bytes; searching for \r\n\r\n
  READING_BODY     — headers parsed; collecting body bytes
  PROCESSING       — request complete; generating response
  WRITING_RESPONSE — write buffer draining to socket
  KEEP_ALIVE       — response sent; waiting for next request
  CLOSING          — teardown in progress; no callbacks
Transitions:
  READING_HEADERS  --[http_try_parse: body present]-----------> READING_BODY
  READING_HEADERS  --[http_try_parse: no body]----------------> PROCESSING
  READING_BODY     --[http_try_parse: body complete]-----------> PROCESSING
  PROCESSING       --[http_process_request called]-------------> WRITING_RESPONSE
  WRITING_RESPONSE --[write_buf drained, keep_alive=1]---------> READING_HEADERS
                     (via KEEP_ALIVE intermediate for edge-only APIs; collapsed here)
  WRITING_RESPONSE --[write_buf drained, keep_alive=0]---------> CLOSING
  READING_HEADERS  --[REACTOR_ERROR or EOF or parse error]-----> CLOSING
  READING_BODY     --[REACTOR_ERROR or EOF]--------------------> CLOSING
  WRITING_RESPONSE --[REACTOR_ERROR or write error]-----------> CLOSING
  any state        --[idle timeout fires]----------------------> CLOSING
  READING_HEADERS  --[header timeout fires]--------------------> CLOSING (via 408 + write + close)
  CLOSING          --[any event]------------------------------> CLOSING (no-op guard)
ILLEGAL transitions (must not occur):
  CLOSING → any non-CLOSING state  (double-close bug)
  PROCESSING → READING_HEADERS without transitioning through WRITING_RESPONSE
  READING_BODY → READING_HEADERS   (must complete or error)
  WRITING_RESPONSE → PROCESSING    (response already started; cannot re-process)
  any state → WRITING_RESPONSE except via http_process_request or http_send_error
```
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-34}}
### Timer Lifecycle per Connection
```
Events:         [conn_new]  [data received]  [conn_close]  [timeout fires]
idle_timer:
  ABSENT        --[conn_new + set_timeout]--> ACTIVE
  ACTIVE        --[data received: cancel + set_timeout]--> ACTIVE (reset)
  ACTIVE        --[conn_close: cancel_timer]--> ABSENT
  ACTIVE        --[timer fires: timer_id=-1]--> ABSENT → conn_close called
header_timer:
  ABSENT        --[conn_new + set_timeout]--> ACTIVE
  ACTIVE        --[headers complete: cancel_timer]--> ABSENT
  ACTIVE        --[timer fires: 408 + close]--> ABSENT
  ACTIVE        --[conn_close before headers: cancel_timer]--> ABSENT
INVARIANT: after http_conn_close returns, both timer_ids are -1.
           There are no dangling timer references to freed connections.
```

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Static File Serving Path: resolve_path(), MIME Detection, and Response Construction
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Flowchart starting at http_process_request(). Step 1: Check method — not GET/HEAD → http_send_error(405). Step 2: resolve_path(serve_root, req->path, real_path). sub-steps: snprintf(candidate = serve_root + req_path). realpath(candidate) → resolves symlinks and '..'. strncmp(result, serve_root, root_len) — if no match (path escaped root) → http_send_error(404). If candidate is directory → append /index.html → realpath again. Step 3: open(real_path, O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC). fstat() — if not S_ISREG → 404. Step 4: get_mime_type(real_path) — strrchr for extension, linear scan of MIME_TABLE. Step 5: snprintf HTTP/1.1 200 OK header with Content-Type, Content-Length, Connection. Step 6: http_write_append(header_buf). Step 7: read file contents into write buffer. Step 8: http_handle_write() — flush immediately. Annotation on path traversal: 'GET /../../../etc/passwd → realpath resolves to /etc/passwd → strncmp fails → 404'.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: HTTP/1.1 Keep-Alive Connection Reuse: State Reset Sequence
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Sequence diagram between client, reactor, and http_conn_t. Request 1 lifecycle: client sends GET /index.html. REACTOR_READ fires. READING_HEADERS → PROCESSING → WRITING_RESPONSE. write buffer drains. check keep_alive==1. STATE RESET: state=READING_HEADERS, read_len=0, write_offset=0, write_len=0, memset(&request). Cancel idle timer, re-arm idle timer (30s). State is now ready for request 2. Request 2 lifecycle: client sends GET /style.css on same fd. REACTOR_READ fires. Same flow. After response: check Connection: close header → keep_alive=0. Write buffer drains. STATE: CLOSING. http_conn_close(). Annotation: 'the fd never closes between requests in keep-alive; OS never creates a new TCP connection; TCP three-way handshake cost paid once for N requests'.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: HTTP Pipelining: Buffered Next-Request Handling via reactor_defer
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Sequence diagram. Client sends two requests back-to-back without waiting. Both arrive in one TCP segment. REACTOR_READ: read() returns 850 bytes. http_try_parse() finds request 1 complete at byte 400, state→PROCESSING. http_process_request() builds response, appends to write_buf. http_handle_write() flushes. State reset for keep-alive: read_len = 850-400 = 450 (request 2 bytes already in buffer). if read_len > 0: reactor_defer(http_deferred_parse, conn). PHASE 3: http_deferred_parse() called. http_try_parse() on remaining 450 bytes → request 2 complete. http_process_request() for request 2. Alternative (WRONG) path shown in red: calling http_try_parse directly inside http_handle_write → recursion → handle_write inside handle_write → stack depth unbounded for deeply pipelined clients.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Two-Tier Timeout Protection: Idle Timer vs Header Deadline Timer
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Timeline for three connection scenarios. SCENARIO A (normal): connect at T=0. idle_timer armed at T+30s. header_timer armed at T+10s. GET request arrives at T=2s. header_timer cancelled. idle_timer reset to T+32s. Response sent. idle_timer reset to T+62s (keep-alive wait). T+62s: idle_timer fires, connection closed. SCENARIO B (slow loris): connect at T=0. Sends 1 byte at T=9s (resets idle timer). header_timer fires at T=10s regardless of recent byte. 408 sent, connection closed. SCENARIO C (zombie / no data): connect at T=0. header_timer fires at T=10s. 408 sent, connection closed. Annotation: 'idle_timer alone cannot stop slow loris; header_timer is the defense — fires on wall-clock deadline from connection open, not from last-byte-received'.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Resource Cleanup on Connection Close: All Exit Paths and Cleanup Order
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Five exit path swimlanes, all converging on the same cleanup sequence. Exit paths: (1) Normal close after response (keep_alive=0). (2) Idle timeout callback fires. (3) Header timeout callback fires. (4) Client EOF (read() returns 0). (5) REACTOR_ERROR. All paths converge at http_conn_close(). Guard: if state==CLOSING return immediately (prevents double-free when idle timer and REACTOR_ERROR occur in same tick). Cleanup sequence (ordered): state=CLOSING → reactor_cancel_timer(idle_timer_id) → reactor_cancel_timer(header_timer_id) → reactor_deregister(reactor, fd) [deferred if inside dispatch] → close(fd) → free(write_buf) → free(conn). Annotations: 'cancel timers FIRST to prevent re-entry from timer callback after fd is closed'. 'reactor_deregister before close(fd) to prevent fd reuse race'. 'free(conn) last — conn fields accessed until final line'.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: C10K Benchmark Architecture: wrk Load Profile and Kernel Buffer Pipeline
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: System diagram showing wrk (12 threads, 10000 connections) on the left, HTTP server (1 thread) on the right, connected via loopback. Inside the server: epoll readiness queue shown with ~50 active fds (colored) out of 10000 registered (gray). Per-tick processing annotation: 'epoll_wait returns 200 events → 200 × http_io_callback calls → 200 file open/stat/read → 200 response writes → 200 timer resets'. Memory breakdown box: 10000 × 26KB http_conn_t = 260MB. 10000 × 65536 write_buf = 640MB (worst case all buffered). Expected benchmark output box: 'Latency p50 ~1.5ms, p99 ~12ms, p99.9 ~45ms. Throughput 100K req/sec. Errors: 0'. System tuning checklist: ulimit -n 65536, somaxconn=65536, tcp_max_syn_backlog=65536, ip_local_port_range=1024-65535, tcp_tw_reuse=1.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Hardware Soul: Full Request Hot Path Annotated with Cycle and Cache Costs
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Annotated call graph for one HTTP GET /index.html request from REACTOR_READ event to write() completion. Each step annotated with CPU cost. epoll_wait wake [NIC interrupt → context switch: 0 cycles while sleeping]. conn lookup handlers[fd] [L3 miss if cold: 200 cycles, L1 hit if warm: 4 cycles]. http_io_callback call [indirect branch through function pointer: ~5 cycles, well-predicted]. read() syscall [user→kernel ring: 150 cycles + memcpy 500-byte request: ~500 cycles]. find_header_end() scan [sequential 500 bytes: ~500 cycles, all in L1]. parse_request_line() [~200 cycles]. parse_headers() [~500 cycles]. open()+fstat() [syscalls: ~3000-5000 cycles each if inode cached; 100μs if cold]. read(file) [memcpy 100-byte file: ~100 cycles from page cache]. snprintf response header [~300 cycles]. write() syscall [150 cycles + kernel memcpy to send buffer: ~100 cycles]. Total hot-path (warm cache, small file): ~10,000–15,000 cycles ≈ 4–6 μs at 2.5 GHz. TLB pressure annotation: 10K × 26KB conn_t structs span ~65K 4KB pages; L2 TLB on typical x86 covers ~1500 entries; expect frequent TLB misses for cold connections. Mitigation: mmap with MAP_HUGETLB.

criteria: Create the epoll instance using epoll_create1(EPOLL_CLOEXEC) and verify epfd >= 0; never use the deprecated epoll_create() without CLOEXEC
Set the listening socket to non-blocking mode at creation time using SOCK_NONBLOCK flag in socket() call, or via fcntl(F_SETFL, O_NONBLOCK) if using the two-step approach
Set all client sockets to non-blocking mode using accept4() with SOCK_NONBLOCK | SOCK_CLOEXEC flags — a single atomic syscall rather than accept() + fcntl()
Register the listening socket with epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_ADD) for EPOLLIN events before entering the event loop
Implement the accept loop: call accept4() in a while(1) loop, break on EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK, continue on ECONNABORTED, and register each accepted fd with epoll before returning
Implement level-triggered (LT) mode event loop: read one chunk per EPOLLIN event, return to epoll_wait() without draining; verify epoll re-notifies if data remains in the receive buffer

Implement edge-triggered (ET) mode event loop by adding EPOLLET to the event flags; read in a while(1) loop inside the event handler, breaking only on EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK to fully drain the kernel receive buffer
Demonstrate the ET silent data loss bug: a version that reads only once per ET event loses data when a client sends more than one buffer-size worth of data in a single burst
Treat EAGAIN and EWOULDBLOCK as non-errors on all read() and write() calls — neither should close the connection or print an error
Handle EPOLLERR and EPOLLHUP events by calling epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_DEL) before close(fd) to avoid fd-reuse bugs
Allocate per-connection state using a flat array indexed by file descriptor number; conn_new() initializes state and conn_free() zeroes it on close
Echo server correctly echoes all received bytes back to the client in both LT and ET modes, verified with payloads larger than the read buffer size (> 4096 bytes)
Handle EINTR from epoll_wait() with a continue, not an error exit
Call epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_DEL, fd, NULL) before close(fd) on every connection teardown path (error, EOF, EPOLLHUP)
When write() returns EAGAIN (errno EAGAIN or EWOULDBLOCK) on a non-blocking socket, all unsent bytes are appended to a per-connection write buffer and no bytes are silently dropped
When write() returns a positive value less than len (partial write), the write_offset is advanced by the returned count and the remaining bytes are queued in the write buffer
EPOLLOUT is registered via epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_MOD) only when the write buffer transitions from empty to non-empty; it is never set in the initial EPOLL_CTL_ADD registration

When conn_flush_write_buf() drains the write buffer completely (write_buf_pending() == 0), epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_MOD) is called to remove EPOLLOUT from the interest mask, preventing the busy-loop anti-pattern
The epollout_armed flag is maintained as canonical truth: arm/disarm functions check this flag before calling epoll_ctl to prevent double-registration and double-deregistration
When the write buffer already contains pending data, conn_write() appends new data to the buffer rather than attempting a direct write(), preserving TCP byte ordering

A min-heap timer data structure is implemented with correct sift-up and sift-down operations; heap_swap() updates both heap_idx in the entries array and timer_id in the corresponding conn_t simultaneously

timer_insert() inserts a new timer in O(log n) time using CLOCK_MONOTONIC for expiry timestamps; timer_cancel() removes a timer in O(log n) time using the swap-with-last technique followed by sift-up and sift-down
On each EPOLLIN event where data is received, timer_reset() cancels the connection's existing timer and inserts a new one with expiry = now_ms() + IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS (30 seconds)
compute_epoll_timeout() returns -1 when the heap is empty, 0 when the earliest timer is already overdue, and (expiry_ms - now_ms()) otherwise; this value is passed directly to epoll_wait()
timer_expire_all() uses a while loop (not if) to process ALL timers with expiry_ms <= now_ms() after every epoll_wait() return, calling conn_close() for each expired connection

conn_close() cancels the connection's timer (if timer_id >= 0), calls epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_DEL) before close(), frees the write_buf_t heap allocation, and clears the conn_t entry — all steps in every close path including error and timeout

After handling EPOLLIN in the event dispatch loop, the conn_t state is re-checked before handling EPOLLOUT on the same fd, preventing write operations on a connection that the read handler already closed
epoll_wait() returning with errno == EINTR causes the loop to call timer_expire_all() and retry rather than exiting
Idle connections that send no data for 30 seconds are closed: verified by connecting with netcat, waiting 30 seconds without sending data, and observing the server close the connection
Implement reactor_t struct with epoll fd, fd_handler_t array indexed by fd number, min-heap timer structures, ring-buffer deferred task queue, and pending_ops array for deferred epoll modifications

reactor_register(r, fd, events, cb, udata) adds or modifies an fd's interest using REACTOR_READ | REACTOR_WRITE flags; when called during dispatch (dispatching==1), enqueues PENDING_ADD or PENDING_MOD instead of calling epoll_ctl directly

reactor_deregister(r, fd) marks fd_handler_t.closing=1 and enqueues PENDING_DEL when called during dispatch; when called outside dispatch, immediately calls epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_DEL) and clears the handler slot

Dispatch loop sets dispatching=1 before iterating events, sets dispatching=0 after, skips events for fds where h->closing==1 or h->registered==0, and translates EPOLLIN/EPOLLOUT/EPOLLERR/EPOLLHUP to REACTOR_READ/REACTOR_WRITE/REACTOR_ERROR before invoking callbacks
reactor_set_timeout(r, delay_ms, cb, udata) schedules a one-shot timer returning a non-negative timer_id; timer fires exactly once after delay_ms milliseconds using CLOCK_MONOTONIC

reactor_set_interval(r, interval_ms, cb, udata) schedules a repeating timer returning a non-negative timer_id; timer reschedules itself before invoking the callback to handle self-cancellation correctly
reactor_cancel_timer(r, timer_id) removes the timer from the min-heap using swap-with-last plus sift-up/sift-down; returns 0 on success, -1 if timer_id not found

reactor_defer(r, cb, udata) enqueues cb to a ring buffer; deferred callbacks run in Phase 3 (after Phase 1 I/O dispatch and Phase 2 pending-ops application); callbacks added during Phase 3 run in the NEXT tick via drain_until snapshot

Four-phase dispatch loop executes in order: Phase 1 (I/O dispatch with dispatching=1), Phase 2 (apply pending_ops: PENDING_ADD/MOD via epoll_ctl, PENDING_DEL clears handler slot), Phase 3 (drain deferred queue up to snapshot), Phase 4 (expire all timers whose expiry_ms <= now_ms())
epoll_wait timeout is computed as max(0, nearest_timer_expiry_ms - now_ms()); returns -1 (infinite) when no timers are pending
EPOLLHUP and EPOLLERR kernel events are always delivered to the registered callback as REACTOR_ERROR regardless of whether the user subscribed to them

User code (echo server or HTTP server) contains zero epoll symbols: no epoll_ctl, no epoll_wait, no EPOLLIN/EPOLLOUT/EPOLLET constants; all I/O registration goes through reactor_register/reactor_deregister
reactor_run(r) blocks until reactor_stop(r) is called; reactor_stop sets running=0 which causes the loop to exit after the current tick completes
Demonstrate re-entrancy safety: a callback that calls reactor_deregister on its own fd does not cause use-after-free or processing of subsequent events for that fd in the same epoll_wait batch
All registered callbacks receive (int fd, uint32_t events, void *udata) arguments where udata is the pointer passed to reactor_register, unchanged

Implement an incremental HTTP parser that accumulates bytes in conn->read_buf and calls http_try_parse() after each read(), returning PARSE_INCOMPLETE (not an error) when headers have not yet arrived completely — verified by sending a request one byte at a time and confirming correct response

Implement the six-state HTTP connection state machine: HTTP_STATE_READING_HEADERS, HTTP_STATE_READING_BODY, HTTP_STATE_PROCESSING, HTTP_STATE_WRITING_RESPONSE, HTTP_STATE_KEEP_ALIVE, HTTP_STATE_CLOSING — each state transition must be explicit and all six states must be reachable in normal operation
Detect the HTTP header-body boundary by scanning for \r\n\r\n in the accumulated read buffer; correctly handle requests where this delimiter spans multiple read() calls

Parse the HTTP request line (method, path, HTTP version) and all header fields (Content-Length, Connection, and arbitrary headers) from the accumulated buffer using null-terminated storage in conn->header_buf

Handle partial writes using the write buffer from M2: when write() returns EAGAIN or a short count, buffer remaining bytes, call reactor_register() to add REACTOR_WRITE interest, and deregister REACTOR_WRITE via reactor_register() with REACTOR_READ only when the buffer drains completely

Implement HTTP/1.1 keep-alive: after flushing the response, if request.keep_alive == 1, reset conn->state to HTTP_STATE_READING_HEADERS, zero conn->read_len, conn->write_offset, conn->write_len, and conn->request, and reset the idle timer — connection fd must NOT be closed
Implement Connection: close semantics: when request.keep_alive == 0 (HTTP/1.0 default, or Connection: close header present), close the connection after the response is fully sent

Implement pipelining: if conn->read_len > 0 after transitioning to KEEP_ALIVE state (a subsequent request arrived while the previous response was being sent), schedule parsing via reactor_defer() and process the buffered request in Phase 3 without waiting for another REACTOR_READ event

Implement a 10-second header deadline timer (header_timer_id) separate from the idle timer: fire http_header_timeout_cb() if headers are not fully received within 10 seconds of connection creation, send a 408 response and close

Implement a 30-second idle timeout timer (idle_timer_id) that resets on every REACTOR_READ event with received data, and fires http_idle_timeout_cb() to close the connection if no data arrives for 30 seconds

Serve static files from a configured serve root directory: open the file with O_RDONLY | O_CLOEXEC, call fstat() to get Content-Length, detect MIME type from file extension using a lookup table with at least 8 entries, and send correct HTTP/1.1 response headers
Implement path sanitization using realpath() to resolve the requested path and verify it starts with the configured serve root — reject paths that escape the root with a 404 response

Return appropriate HTTP error responses: 400 Bad Request for malformed requests, 404 Not Found for missing files, 405 Method Not Allowed for non-GET/HEAD methods, 408 Request Timeout on header deadline, 413 Request Entity Too Large when the read buffer fills before headers complete

Implement complete connection cleanup on all exit paths (client EOF, REACTOR_ERROR, idle timeout, header timeout, write buffer overflow): cancel idle_timer_id, cancel header_timer_id, call reactor_deregister(), close(fd), free(conn->write_buf), free(conn) — in that order, with a state == HTTP_STATE_CLOSING guard to prevent double-free

Benchmark with wrk or ab demonstrating 10,000+ concurrent connections with p99 latency under 100ms for a small static file response — report wrk -t12 -c10000 -d60s --latency output including Requests/sec and latency percentiles
Increase system limits before benchmarking: ulimit -n 65536, and verify the server runs without 'too many open files' errors at 10K connections

The accept loop calls accept4() with SOCK_NONBLOCK | SOCK_CLOEXEC in a loop until EAGAIN, allocates http_conn_t with calloc(), arms both idle and header timers, and calls reactor_register() for REACTOR_READ — dropping connections gracefully when allocation fails
Zero wrk-reported errors (connection resets, timeouts, non-2xx responses) during the 60-second benchmark run at 10K concurrent connections serving a static file
Create epoll instance with epoll_create1(EPOLL_CLOEXEC) and register the listening socket for EPOLLIN events
Set ALL sockets (listening and client) to non-blocking mode using SOCK_NONBLOCK flag on socket() and accept4()
Accept new connections in a loop calling accept4() until EAGAIN, registering each client fd with epoll
Implement level-triggered (LT) mode: read one chunk per EPOLLIN event; verify epoll re-notifies when data remains unread
Implement edge-triggered (ET) mode with EPOLLET flag: read in a loop until EAGAIN to drain the socket completely
Demonstrate the ET correctness requirement: reading only once per event in ET mode loses data for payloads > BUF_SIZE
Handle EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK on all non-blocking read() and write() calls without treating them as errors
Handle ECONNABORTED on accept4() by continuing the accept loop rather than exiting
Handle EINTR on epoll_wait() by retrying rather than treating it as a fatal error
Handle EPOLLERR and EPOLLHUP by calling conn_close() to tear down the connection cleanly
Call epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_DEL) before close(fd) for every connection teardown to avoid fd-reuse races
Build a basic echo server using the event loop: read data from client, write it back
Use a flat array conn_t[MAX_CONNS] indexed by file descriptor number to store per-connection state
conn_new() initializes a slot to CONN_STATE_ACTIVE; conn_free() resets it to CONN_STATE_FREE (zero)
Drop connections gracefully when fd >= MAX_CONNS rather than crashing or corrupting the table
Pass integration test: LT and ET modes both correctly echo a 32KB payload (8x the 4KB read buffer)
Create epoll instance with epoll_create1(EPOLL_CLOEXEC) and register the listening socket for EPOLLIN events
Set ALL sockets (listening and client) to non-blocking mode using fcntl(O_NONBLOCK)
Accept new connections in a loop (accept until EAGAIN) and register each with epoll
Implement level-triggered (LT) mode first: epoll re-notifies if data remains unread; read can process one chunk per event
Then implement edge-triggered (ET) mode (EPOLLET flag): read in a loop until EAGAIN to drain the socket completely, because epoll will NOT re-notify for already-available data
Demonstrate the difference: in ET mode, reading only once per event loses data; in LT mode, it works but may cause redundant wakeups
Handle EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK on all non-blocking read() and write() calls without treating them as errors
Build a basic echo server as the test application: read data from client, echo it back
Use an array or hash map indexed by file descriptor to store per-connection state
When write() returns EAGAIN (socket send buffer full), buffer the remaining data in a per-connection write queue
Register interest in EPOLLOUT for that fd; when EPOLLOUT fires, attempt to flush the write buffer
When the write buffer is fully flushed, DEREGISTER EPOLLOUT interest to avoid busy-spinning on write-ready events
Handle partial writes: write() may send fewer bytes than requested; advance the buffer offset and retry on next EPOLLOUT
Implement timer management using a min-heap (priority queue) or single-level timer wheel with configurable resolution
Support connection idle timeouts: if no data is received for N seconds, close the connection and free its state
Use epoll_wait timeout parameter set to the time until the next timer expiration; when no timers are pending, use -1 (infinite wait)
Timer cancellation: when a connection receives data, reset its idle timer; when a connection closes, cancel its timer
reactor_t struct is opaque: user code includes only reactor.h and never accesses internal fields directly
reactor_register called during dispatch (dispatching==1) enqueues PENDING_ADD or PENDING_MOD and does not call epoll_ctl
reactor_deregister called during dispatch sets handlers[fd].closing=1, enqueues PENDING_DEL, and does not call epoll_ctl
Dispatch loop skips any fd where handlers[fd].closing==1, preventing use-after-free when a callback deregisters its own fd
Phase 2 applies all pending_ops in FIFO order after dispatching is reset to 0; PENDING_DEL clears the handlers[] slot
Phase 3 uses a drain_until snapshot of deferred_tail taken before the drain loop; deferred callbacks added during Phase 3 run in the next tick only
Phase 4 calls reactor_expire_timers after Phase 3; a connection that resets its timer in Phase 1 is NOT expired in Phase 4 of the same tick
reactor_set_timeout callback fires exactly once; the timer_id is invalid after firing and reactor_cancel_timer on it returns -1
reactor_set_interval reschedules to prev_expiry + interval_ms BEFORE calling the callback so self-cancellation from within the callback works correctly
reactor_cancel_timer returns -1 for unknown or already-fired timer IDs without corrupting the heap
reactor_defer returns -1 when the ring buffer is full (MAX_DEFERRED-1 entries pending)
EPOLLERR and EPOLLHUP are always translated to REACTOR_ERROR and delivered to the registered callback regardless of the event mask used at registration
echo_reactor.c contains zero epoll_* symbols (verified by grep)
All registered fds use EPOLLET (edge-triggered); the reactor never registers an fd without the EPOLLET flag
reactor_destroy does not call close() on user-registered fds; it only closes the epoll fd itself
Compile with -O2 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -g with zero warnings

Create http_conn_t struct with read_buf (16KB), write_buf pointer (M2), state enum, two timer IDs (idle + header), epollout_armed flag, and http_request_t with header_buf storage; add _Static_assert for all field offsets

Implement find_header_end() scanning for \r\n\r\n returning pointer to byte after terminator or NULL; implement parse_request_line() extracting method/path/http_minor with PARSE_ERROR on malformed input

Implement parse_headers() with case-insensitive Content-Length and Connection extraction, whitespace trimming, default keep_alive based on HTTP version, storage of name/value pairs into header_buf with null terminators

Implement http_try_parse() as top-level incremental parser: calls find_header_end, parse_request_line, parse_headers in sequence; handles body accumulation; preserves pipelined bytes via memmove after headers consumed; returns PARSE_INCOMPLETE/COMPLETE/ERROR/TOO_LARGE
Implement http_handle_read() with ET drain-until-EAGAIN loop; calls http_try_parse after each read(); resets idle timer on every n>0 read; handles buffer-full (413) and EOF cases

Implement resolve_path() using realpath() to prevent directory traversal; verifies resolved path starts with serve_root followed by '/' or '\0'; appends /index.html for directories; frees realpath() malloc result internally

Implement http_process_request(): cancel header timer; validate method (GET/HEAD only → 405); resolve_path → 404 on failure; open/fstat → 404 if not regular file; build response header with snprintf; append file body in chunks; call http_handle_write

Implement http_handle_write() with write loop until write_buf_pending==0 or EAGAIN; arm EPOLLOUT via reactor_register(REACTOR_READ|REACTOR_WRITE) on EAGAIN; disarm via reactor_register(REACTOR_READ) on drain; on drain with keep_alive=1 reset state/request/timers; on drain with keep_alive=0 call http_conn_close

Implement pipelining: after response sent with keep_alive=1, if conn->read_len>0 call reactor_defer(http_deferred_parse, conn); http_deferred_parse calls http_try_parse and dispatches to http_process_request in Phase 3

Implement two-tier timeouts: http_idle_timeout_cb closes connection after 30s inactivity; http_header_timeout_cb sends 408 after 10s if headers incomplete; both timer IDs set in http_accept_cb; idle resets on every data receipt; header timer cancelled when parse completes

Implement http_conn_close() with state=CLOSING guard as first action for double-close prevention; cancels both timers; calls reactor_deregister; closes fd; uses reactor_defer(http_deferred_free) to free write_buf and conn after current dispatch phase
Benchmark with wrk -t12 -c10000 -d60s --latency; demonstrate 10K concurrent connections; report p99 latency < 100ms; verify zero epoll symbols in http_*.c files via grep
